blocks.x: A letter is allowed to stand for a number you do not know yet, or one that is free to change, and that single move lets one short line of math describe a whole family of situations at once instead of a single frozen case. Numbers and letters stitched together with the usual operations form an expression, like \(3x + 5\). Nothing you learned gets thrown out, since a letter is just a number in disguise. Two conventions to keep for good: \(3x\) means \(3 \times x\), and \(x^2\) means \(x \times x\).

A bead bracelet holds \(3\) colored beads per charm plus \(4\) fixed clasp beads, so a bracelet with \(n\) charms has \(3n + 4\) beads. A customer orders \(6\) charms. Evaluate \(3n + 4\) at \(n = 6\) by replacing \(n\) with \(6\). How many beads are on that bracelet? Type the single number.
hints: The expression is \(3n + 4\). To evaluate at \(n = 6\), swap the letter \(n\) for the number \(6\) everywhere it appears. You should be looking at \(3(6) + 4\).
Do the multiplication first. \(3(6)\) means \(3 \times 6\), which is \(18\). Then add the \(4\) clasp beads.
blocks.sol: The total number of beads on a bracelet with \(n\) charms is the expression $$3n + 4.$$ To handle the order for \(6\) charms, evaluate at \(n = 6\) by replacing every \(n\) with \(6\). $$3(6) + 4 = 18 + 4 = 22.$$ The bracelet has \(\boxed{22}\) beads.

blocks.x: Evaluating an expression. Replace every letter with the given value and finish the arithmetic. The expression on its own answers the general question, describing every case at once. The moment you want one specific case back, plugging in a number collapses the whole thing into ordinary arithmetic. Build the expression once, then evaluate it as many times as you like.

A diver's height in meters is \(11 - 4t\), where \(t\) counts minutes of descent. An expression splits into terms separated by signs, here \(11\) and \(-4t\). The number multiplying \(t\), carried with its sign, is the coefficient of \(t\). Read off the coefficient of \(t\) and type that single value, sign included.
hints: A subtraction is really an addition of a negative. Rewrite \(11 - 4t\) as \(11 + (-4t)\) so the sign on the \(t\) term sits right out in the open.
The coefficient of \(t\) is the whole number multiplying \(t\), the minus included. In \(-4t\) that number is \(-4\).
blocks.sol: Start by splitting the expression into its terms. Reading \(11 - 4t\) as a sum makes the signs honest, \(11 + (-4t)\). Now the two terms are \(11\) and \(-4t\). The coefficient of \(t\) is the number multiplying \(t\), carried along with its sign, so it is \(-4\). The lone \(11\) is the constant term, the diver's starting height, and it is not attached to \(t\) at all. The coefficient of \(t\) is \(\boxed{-4}\).

blocks.x: Terms, coefficients, like terms. A term is a number stuck to a variable, like \(4x\); that front number with its sign is the coefficient, so a lone \(x\) means \(1x\) and a lone \(-x\) means \(-1x\). A plain number alone is a constant. Two terms with the exact same variable part are like terms, and combining them is just counting, since \(x + x = 2x\). The plus and minus signs are the seams between terms, so carry each sign with its term when you tally the coefficients.

blocks.cap: Each tile is a shape, and only matching shapes pile into one count. Four \(x\)-strips and one more \(x\)-strip stack into a single tally of \(5x\). But an \(x^2\) square is not the same shape as an \(x\)-strip, and an \(x\)-strip is not the same letter as a \(y\)-strip, so those tiles sit side by side and stay apart.

blocks.x: Sort by shape before combining. In \(x^2 + 5x + 2x^2 + 3\), the squares belong with squares and the strips with strips. A thing squared and the thing itself are different kinds of object, so \(x^2\) and \(5x\) refuse to merge, which is exactly why counting terms is a real question. Write \(x^2 + 5x + 2x^2 + 3\) in simplest form. How many terms does it have once fully simplified? Type the single count.
hints: Sort the four pieces by their shape before you add anything. Which ones are square tiles \(x^2\), which is a strip \(5x\), and which is a plain number? Only pieces of the same shape can be combined.

Combine just the squares, \(x^2 + 2x^2 = 3x^2\), and leave \(5x\) and \(3\) exactly where they are since nothing matches them. That gives \(3x^2 + 5x + 3\). Now count the separate pieces joined by plus signs.
blocks.sol: Group the like terms by shape. The square tiles combine, \(x^2 + 2x^2 = 3x^2\). The strip \(5x\) is alone in its shape, and the number \(3\) is a constant by itself, so neither one combines with anything. Putting the survivors in order from the highest power down gives $$x^2 + 5x + 2x^2 + 3 = 3x^2 + 5x + 3.$$ Now read off the separate terms joined by plus signs. They are \(3x^2\), then \(5x\), then \(3\). The \(x^2\) part and the \(x\) part stay apart because squaring \(x\) makes a different kind of object than \(x\) itself, so they cannot fold together. That leaves three distinct terms, so the count is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: Only like terms combine. Fold two terms together only when their variable parts match exactly; then add the coefficients and the variable rides along unchanged. If the variable parts differ even slightly, like \(x^2\) next to \(x\), or \(x\) next to \(y\), they are different kinds of thing and no move smushes them into one. That is why the count of terms in simplest form means something: it tells you how many genuinely different quantities are still standing.

Different letters stand for different kinds, so \(x\) and \(y\) never combine. In \(3x + 5y + 2x + y\), gather the \(x\) terms with each other and the \(y\) terms with each other, remembering the lone \(y\) means \(1y\). After you simplify, what is the coefficient of \(x\)? Type that single value.
hints: Sort the terms into two piles before you add anything. One pile holds the \(x\) terms, the other holds the \(y\) terms. A term with \(x\) can never land in the \(y\) pile, so the two letters get counted separately.
Add the \(x\) terms by themselves, \(3x + 2x = 5x\), and leave the \(y\) terms alone. The number sitting in front of \(x\) is its coefficient, so read that off.
blocks.sol: Keep the two letters in separate piles, since \(x\) and \(y\) are different kinds and never combine. Gather the \(x\) terms, \(3x + 2x = 5x\). Gather the \(y\) terms, remembering the lone \(y\) means \(1y\), so \(5y + y = 6y\). Putting the piles side by side, the expression in simplest form is $$3x + 5y + 2x + y = 5x + 6y.$$ The coefficient of \(x\) is the number multiplying it, which is \(5\). So the answer is \(\boxed{5}\).

blocks.x: Nobody used a letter for the unknown until the 1500s. Viète introduced vowels; Descartes shifted to \(x, y, z\), possibly because his printer had plenty of spare \(x\) type blocks.

blocks.cap: Cutting the width into two pieces cuts the area into two pieces, so a rectangle of height \(a\) and width \(b+c\) has the same area as the two slabs \(ab\) and \(ac\) put together. Multiplying a group hands the multiplier to every piece inside, which is why \(7(y+2)\) becomes \(7y+14\), as plainly as cutting the rectangle down the middle.

blocks.x: A school orders supply kits in two batches, \(3(x+5) + 4(x+8)\), where each kit holds \(x\) notebooks plus some pens. Distribute each group on its own, then combine the like terms so the order collapses to (notebooks)\(\,x\,\) plus (pens). How many pens does the order come to? Type that single number.
hints: Open each group separately. The first group \(3(x+5)\) becomes \(3x + 15\), and the second group \(4(x+8)\) becomes \(4x + 32\). The pens are the plain numbers with no \(x\) attached.
Add the two plain-number pieces together. You have \(15\) pens from the first batch and \(32\) pens from the second, so the total number of pens is \(15 + 32\).
blocks.sol: Distribute across each group on its own. The first group gives \(3(x+5) = 3x + 15\), and the second group gives \(4(x+8) = 4x + 32\). Putting them together, the full order is \(3x + 15 + 4x + 32\). Now combine the like terms. The notebook terms join as \(3x + 4x = 7x\), and the pen counts join as \(15 + 32 = 47\). So the order simplifies to $$7x + 47.$$ The plain number with no \(x\) on it is the pen count, the constant term \(47\). The order comes to \(\boxed{47}\) pens.

blocks.x: This one leans on signs, so go slowly. Expand \(5(z - 3) + 3(7 - 2z)\), letting each subtraction ride along with its term and remembering \(3 \times (-2z) = -6z\). Combine the like terms all the way to one cleanest form, then read off the coefficient of \(z\). Type that single value, sign included.
hints: Line up your four pieces, \(5z - 15 + 21 - 6z\), then sort them into z terms and plain numbers. The coefficient of \(z\) is whatever number ends up sitting in front of \(z\) once you have gathered \(5z\) and \(-6z\) together.
Add the z terms by adding their coefficients, \(5 + (-6) = -1\), so the z part becomes \(-z\). Writing \(-z\) is the same as writing \(-1z\), so the coefficient is that \(-1\).
blocks.sol: Distribute each group, letting the subtractions travel with the terms. \(5(z - 3) = 5z - 15\), and \(3(7 - 2z) = 21 - 6z\) since \(3 \times (-2z) = -6z\). That gives \(5z - 15 + 21 - 6z\). Now combine like terms. The z terms are \(5z\) and \(-6z\), and \(5z - 6z = -z\), one short of zero rather than five or eleven. The constants are \(-15\) and \(21\), and \(-15 + 21 = 6\). So the simplest form is \(-z + 6\). Since \(-z\) means \(-1z\), the coefficient of \(z\) is \(\boxed{-1}\).

blocks.x: A subtraction in front of a group flips the sign of everything inside, since the multiplier carries its minus into every piece. Take \(8 - 2(x - 5)\). The \(-2\) lands on both the \(x\) and the \(-5\), so the \(-5\) becomes \(+10\), not \(-10\). Distribute, combine the constants, and the expression collapses to (something)\(\,x\,\) plus a constant. What is the constant term once fully simplified? Type that single value.
hints: The number multiplying the group is the whole \(-2\), not just \(2\). Send it onto both pieces inside, so \(-2 \times x\) and \(-2 \times (-5)\). Watch what happens to that second sign.
A negative times a negative is positive, so \(-2 \times (-5) = +10\). That gives \(8 - 2x + 10\). Add the two loose numbers \(8 + 10\) to get the constant, and read it off.
blocks.sol: The \(-2\) in front of the group multiplies both pieces inside. Distributing it, \(-2 \times x = -2x\), and \(-2 \times (-5) = +10\) because a negative times a negative is positive. So $$8 - 2(x - 5) = 8 - 2x + 10.$$ The trap is that the \(-5\) became \(+10\), not \(-10\). Now combine the two constants, \(8 + 10 = 18\), which leaves $$-2x + 18.$$ The constant term is the part with no variable, so the value is \(\boxed{18}\).

blocks.x: Distribute to every piece. When a number sits in front of a group, hand it to every piece inside, not just the first, so \(3(x+4) = 3x + 12\) with the \(3\) reaching all the way to the back. A subtraction or minus in front of a group is really multiplying by \(-1\), so it flips the sign of every piece: \(-(x-5) = -x + 5\). Almost every distribution slip is one term the multiplier was supposed to touch and got left behind.

Two expressions are equivalent when they agree for every value of the variable. Staring at them is not proof, so here is the grading trick: build the difference \(A - B\) and plug in a value. If they truly are equivalent, that difference must come out \(0\). Let \(A = 2(3x+4) + x\) and \(B = 7x + 8\). Compute \(A - B\) at \(x = 5\) and type the single number you get.
hints: Evaluating at \(x = 5\) means replacing every \(x\) with \(5\). Find the value of \(A = 2(3x + 4) + x\) at \(x = 5\), then the value of \(B = 7x + 8\) at \(x = 5\), and finally subtract the second from the first.
blocks.sol: First simplify \(A\) so you can see the twins clearly. Distributing the \(2\) gives \(2(3x + 4) = 6x + 8\), and adding the outside \(x\) gives \(6x + 8 + x = 7x + 8\). So \(A = 7x + 8\), which is identical to \(B = 7x + 8\). The difference simplifies all the way down, since \(A - B = (7x + 8) - (7x + 8) = 0\). That \(0\) is not an accident of one lucky number. The \(x\) terms cancel and the constants cancel, so the difference is \(0\) for every value of \(x\). Now plug in the requested value. At \(x = 5\), \(A = 2(3 \cdot 5 + 4) + 5 = 2 \cdot 19 + 5 = 43\) and \(B = 7 \cdot 5 + 8 = 43\), so the difference is \(43 - 43 = 0\). The two expressions truly are equivalent. \(\boxed{0}\)

blocks.x: Equivalence. Testing values is lopsided. If two expressions disagree even once, you are done, they cannot be equivalent. But matching once, or even a few times, proves nothing, since two different expressions can line up at a lucky value. The only sure way is to rewrite both to the same simplest form. Reordering a sum keeps the meaning, so \(x + 7\) and \(7 + x\) are the same, but \(x - 7\) and \(7 - x\) are not, because subtraction is not symmetric.

The target is \(6n + 10\), and four suspects line up beside it: \(2(3n+5)\), \(3(2n+4)\), \(5 + 6n + 5\), and \(10 + 6n\). Simplify each one until you can compare it cleanly with \(6n + 10\). How many of the four are equivalent to \(6n + 10\)? Type the single count.
hints: To unmask a suspect, rewrite it in the same plain form as the target, which is some number of \(n\) plus a constant. For the ones with parentheses, multiply the outside number by each piece inside. For the ones that are already a string of terms, gather the loose numbers together.

Suspect one becomes \(6n + 10\), a match. Suspect two becomes \(6n + 12\), so its constant is off by 2 and it fails. Suspect three has \(5 + 5 = 10\) so it is \(6n + 10\), a match. Suspect four is \(10 + 6n\), the very same two pieces just written in the other order, which is still a match. Now count the matches.
blocks.sol: Rewrite each suspect in the form "number times \(n\), plus a constant" and compare it with the target \(6n + 10\). Suspect one distributes as \(2(3n + 5) = 6n + 10\), which matches the target exactly. Suspect two distributes as \(3(2n + 4) = 6n + 12\), and since \(12 \ne 10\) the constant is wrong, so this one is an impostor. Suspect three combines its loose numbers, \(5 + 6n + 5 = 6n + 10\), which matches. Suspect four is \(10 + 6n\), and reordering a sum never changes its value, so \(10 + 6n = 6n + 10\), another match. Three of the four suspects simplify to \(6n + 10\) and one does not, so the count of equivalent expressions is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: Coefficients do not have to be whole numbers. A fraction can sit in front of a variable, and combining such terms is the same common-denominator move from Chapter 4 with an \(x\) riding along. The terms \(\tfrac{2x}{3}\) and \(\tfrac{5x}{7}\) are like terms, since both carry a single \(x\). Combine \(\tfrac{2x}{3} + \tfrac{5x}{7}\) into one term, then state the coefficient of \(x\) as a fraction in lowest terms, typed as a over b.
hints: Both terms are like terms, so think of this as adding the coefficients \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{7}\) while the \(x\) just rides along. To add those fractions you need a common denominator for \(3\) and \(7\).

A common denominator of \(3\) and \(7\) is \(21\). Rewrite \(\tfrac{2x}{3}\) as \(\tfrac{14x}{21}\) and \(\tfrac{5x}{7}\) as \(\tfrac{15x}{21}\), then add the numerators over \(21\). The coefficient of \(x\) is whatever fraction lands in front.
blocks.sol: A common denominator of \(3\) and \(7\) is \(21\). Rewrite each term with denominator \(21\), so \(\tfrac{2x}{3} = \tfrac{14x}{21}\) and \(\tfrac{5x}{7} = \tfrac{15x}{21}\). Now add the numerators over the shared denominator. $$\frac{2x}{3} + \frac{5x}{7} = \frac{14x}{21} + \frac{15x}{21} = \frac{29x}{21}$$ That single term is \(\tfrac{29x}{21}\), and its coefficient of \(x\) is \(\tfrac{29}{21}\). Since \(29\) is prime and does not divide \(21\), the fraction is already in lowest terms. The coefficient of \(x\) is \(\boxed{29/21}\).

blocks.x: Fractional coefficients. A fraction in front of a letter never starts a new kind of math: \(\tfrac{2x}{3}\) is just \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of an \(x\). Adding two such terms is the same common-denominator work you already know, with the \(x\) riding along untouched. Find the shared bottom, rebuild each coefficient over it, add the numerators, and set the \(x\) back down beside the answer. Every fraction skill from earlier still works with the letter in the seat.

Sometimes the pieces you are adding live over different denominators, and one piece can be an expression. Combine \(\tfrac{a}{2} + \tfrac{6a - 5}{4}\) onto a common bottom, then write the result as a coefficient of \(a\) plus a constant, like \(2a - \tfrac{5}{4}\). In that clean form, type the constant term as a fraction in lowest terms, with its sign.
hints: The two denominators are \(2\) and \(4\), and \(4\) is a multiple of \(2\), so \(4\) is the common bottom. Rewrite \(\tfrac{a}{2}\) over \(4\) by doubling top and bottom, and leave \(\tfrac{6a-5}{4}\) exactly as it is. Then add the numerators over that one shared \(4\).

You have \(\tfrac{2a}{4} + \tfrac{6a-5}{4} = \tfrac{2a + 6a - 5}{4} = \tfrac{8a-5}{4}\). Split that into two fractions, \(\tfrac{8a}{4} - \tfrac{5}{4} = 2a - \tfrac{5}{4}\). The constant term is the piece with no \(a\) attached.
blocks.sol: The denominators are \(2\) and \(4\). Since \(4\) is a multiple of \(2\), the common denominator is \(4\). Rewrite the first piece by multiplying its top and bottom by \(2\), which turns \(\tfrac{a}{2}\) into \(\tfrac{2a}{4}\). The second piece already sits over \(4\), so leave it alone. Now both pieces share a bottom, so add the numerators over that single \(4\). $$\tfrac{a}{2} + \tfrac{6a - 5}{4} = \tfrac{2a}{4} + \tfrac{6a - 5}{4} = \tfrac{2a + 6a - 5}{4} = \tfrac{8a - 5}{4}$$ To read off the parts, split the single fraction back into a term with \(a\) and a term without. $$\tfrac{8a - 5}{4} = \tfrac{8a}{4} - \tfrac{5}{4} = 2a - \tfrac{5}{4}$$ The coefficient of \(a\) is \(2\), and the constant term is the piece carrying no \(a\), which is \(-\tfrac{5}{4}\). The fraction \(\tfrac{5}{4}\) is already in lowest terms, so the constant term is \(\boxed{-5/4}\).

blocks.x: Here is the whole lesson in one expression: a piece scaled by a fraction, a piece scaled by a whole number, and a piece over a denominator. Simplify \(\frac{1}{2}(4x + 6) + 3(x - 1) + \frac{x - 5}{3}\) all the way down to the form (something)\(\,x\,\) plus (something), then report the coefficient of \(x\) as a fraction in lowest terms, typed as a over b.
hints: Knock out one group at a time before you mix anything. Half of \(4x + 6\) gives \(2x + 3\). Three times \(x - 1\) gives \(3x - 3\), and watch that the \(3\) hits the \(-1\) too. The last group \(\frac{x - 5}{3}\) is one quantity divided by \(3\), so it splits into \(\frac{x}{3} - \frac{5}{3}\).

Now sweep up only the \(x\) terms and ignore the constants for this question. You have \(2x\), \(3x\), and \(\frac{x}{3}\), so the coefficient of \(x\) is \(2 + 3 + \frac{1}{3}\). Put \(2 + 3 = 5\) over a denominator of \(3\) to add the last piece, which makes \(\frac{15}{3} + \frac{1}{3}\).
blocks.sol: Take the three groups one at a time. The first group is \(\frac{1}{2}(4x + 6) = 2x + 3\). The second is \(3(x - 1) = 3x - 3\), keeping the sign on the \(-3\). The third is \(\frac{x - 5}{3} = \frac{x}{3} - \frac{5}{3}\). Lining them up gives \(2x + 3 + 3x - 3 + \frac{x}{3} - \frac{5}{3}\). Gather the \(x\) terms first. Their coefficients add as \(2 + 3 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{15}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{16}{3}\). The constants come out to \(3 - 3 - \frac{5}{3} = -\frac{5}{3}\). So the fully simplified expression is \(\frac{16}{3}x - \frac{5}{3}\), and the coefficient of \(x\) is \(\frac{16}{3}\), already in lowest terms since \(16\) and \(3\) share no factor. The answer is \(\boxed{16/3}\).

review.x: A weather balloon's altitude in meters is \(13 - 7p\), where \(p\) is the number of minutes a valve has been venting. The number multiplying a variable, carried with its sign, is its coefficient. State the coefficient of \(p\) and type that single value, sign included.
hints: The expression \(13 - 7p\) has two pieces, a plain number \(13\) and a term built from \(p\). The coefficient lives in the term that contains \(p\).
The minus sign in front of \(7p\) belongs to the coefficient. So the term is \(-7p\), and the number multiplying \(p\) is not just \(7\).
review.sol: The expression is already in simplest form, \(13 - 7p\). It has two terms, the constant \(13\) and the variable term \(-7p\). Reading the subtraction as adding a negative, the term built from \(p\) is \(-7p\), which means \(-7\) times \(p\). The coefficient of \(p\) is the number multiplying it, sign and all, so the coefficient is \(\boxed{-7}\).

review.x: A print run is modeled by \(19 - 5q\), where \(q\) counts paper jams. The piece \(5q\) shifts as jams shift, but one piece does not change at all. That unchanging number is the constant term. State the constant term of \(19 - 5q\) and type that single number.
hints: The expression \(19 - 5q\) has two pieces. One piece has the letter \(q\) attached to it, and one piece is just a plain number with no letter.
The constant term is the part that stays the same no matter what \(q\) is. The \(5q\) piece depends on \(q\), so the constant term has to be the other piece.
review.sol: The expression is already written in simplest form as \(19 - 5q\). It is built from two terms. The term \(5q\) depends on the variable \(q\), since \(5q\) means \(5\) times \(q\), so its value shifts whenever the number of jams shifts. The other term is just \(19\), a plain number with no variable attached. The constant term is the term with no variable, which is the part that stays fixed no matter what \(q\) equals. You can see this fixed value by imagining zero jams, where \(q = 0\) gives \(19 - 5(0) = 19\). So the constant term is \(\boxed{19}\).

review.x: A lone variable carries a hidden \(1\) in front, so \(m\) means \(1m\). Combine the like terms in \(3m + m + 5m\) into a single term, then type the coefficient of \(m\).
hints: Rewrite the lone \(m\) as \(1m\) so every term shows its number out front, giving \(3m + 1m + 5m\).
Add only the numbers in front, since they all count copies of the same \(m\). The coefficient is what sits in front of \(m\) once you finish.
review.sol: Every term here is a number of copies of \(m\), so they are like terms and can be merged. Reading the lone \(m\) as \(1m\), the expression becomes \(3m + 1m + 5m\). Add the numbers in front, \(3 + 1 + 5 = 9\), and keep the \(m\). The simplified expression is \(9m\). The coefficient of \(m\) is \(\boxed{9}\).

review.x: Tally each kind of term on its own, the plain numbers in one pile and the \(x\) terms in another. Simplify \(8 + 2x + 5 + 4x\), then read off the constant term and type that single value.
hints: Sort the four pieces into two groups. The plain numbers \(8\) and \(5\) belong together, and the \(x\) terms \(2x\) and \(4x\) belong together. You can add inside each group on its own.
Add the plain numbers to get the constant part, and add the \(x\) terms to get the \(x\) part. The constant term is the piece that has no \(x\) attached to it.
review.sol: Group the like terms first. The plain numbers are \(8\) and \(5\), and the \(x\) terms are \(2x\) and \(4x\). Combine each group on its own. The numbers give \(8 + 5 = 13\), and the \(x\) terms give \(2x + 4x = 6x\). Putting both groups back together, the expression in simplest form is \(6x + 13\). The constant term is the piece with no \(x\) attached to it, which is \(13\). So the value you type is \(\boxed{13}\).

review.x: Square tiles each cover \(x^2\) and thin tiles each cover \(x\), and the two kinds never combine because \(x^2\) and \(x\) are different objects. Gather each kind in \(5x^2 + 2x + 3x^2 + x\), write it in simplest form, then count how many terms remain.
hints: Sort the pieces into two piles. Put all the square \(x^2\) tiles together and all the thin \(x\) tiles together, then add up each pile on its own.
The \(x^2\) pile gives \(5x^2 + 3x^2 = 8x^2\) and the \(x\) pile gives \(2x + x = 3x\). Once you write those two results side by side, count the separate pieces joined by plus signs.
review.sol: Keep the two kinds of tiles separate while you combine. The square tiles give \(5x^2 + 3x^2 = 8x^2\), and the thin x-tiles give \(2x + x = 3x\). Putting the piles back together, the simplest form is \(8x^2 + 3x\). A term is one chunk separated by a plus or minus sign, so \(8x^2\) is one term and \(3x\) is another. They cannot merge into a single piece because a square tile is not the same kind of tile as a thin one. That leaves two terms. \(\boxed{2}\)

review.x: Since \(a\) and \(b\) stand for different things, an \(a\) term and a \(b\) term can never merge. Combine only the matching kinds in \(6a + 2b + a + 3b\), write the result in simplest form, then type how many terms are left.
hints: Group the like terms by sweeping up all the \(a\) pieces in one bundle and all the \(b\) pieces in another. You have \(6a\) and \(a\), and separately \(2b\) and \(3b\).
Adding the \(a\) pieces gives \(7a\), and adding the \(b\) pieces gives \(5b\). These cannot merge any further, so write the answer as a sum of those two bundles and then count the bundles.
review.sol: Start by collecting the pieces that are the same kind. The \(a\) terms are \(6a\) and \(a\), which together make \(6a + a = 7a\). The \(b\) terms are \(2b\) and \(3b\), which together make \(2b + 3b = 5b\). An \(a\) bundle and a \(b\) bundle measure different objects, so they stay apart. The simplest form is $$6a + 2b + a + 3b = 7a + 5b.$$ Now read off what the question wants. The simplified expression \(7a + 5b\) is made of two separate pieces joined by addition, the \(7a\) and the \(5b\). That is two terms. So the count is \(\boxed{2}\).

review.x: Expand \(8(3x + 2)\) by handing the \(8\) to every piece inside, so it lands on both the \(3x\) and the \(2\). In the result, the number multiplying \(x\) is its coefficient. What is the coefficient of \(x\)? Type that single value.
hints: Hand the \(8\) to each piece on its own. You get \(8 \times 3x\) for the first piece and \(8 \times 2\) for the second piece. Work those out and add them.
After expanding you have one term with an \(x\) and one plain number. The coefficient of \(x\) is just the number stuck to the \(x\), not the plain number at the end.
review.sol: Hand the \(8\) to every piece inside the group. \(8 \times 3x = 24x\) and \(8 \times 2 = 16\). Putting those together, the expanded expression is \(24x + 16\). No parentheses are left, so it is in simplest form. The term holding the \(x\) is \(24x\), and the number multiplying \(x\) is \(24\). That number is the coefficient of \(x\). So the coefficient is \(\boxed{24}\).

review.x: Distribute across each group in \(2(x+6) + 5(x+1)\), then combine the like terms into simplest form. Read off the constant term, the lone number with no \(x\) attached, and type that single value.
hints: Distribute first. \(2(x+6)\) becomes \(2x + 12\), and \(5(x+1)\) becomes \(5x + 5\). Now you have \(2x + 12 + 5x + 5\).
Group the \(x\) terms together and group the plain numbers together. The plain numbers are \(12\) and \(5\). The constant term is whatever they add up to.
review.sol: Distribute each group on its own. The first group gives \(2(x+6) = 2x + 12\), and the second gives \(5(x+1) = 5x + 5\). Putting those together, the expression is \(2x + 12 + 5x + 5\). Now combine like terms. The \(x\) terms are \(2x\) and \(5x\), which add to \(7x\). The plain numbers are \(12\) and \(5\), which add to \(17\). So in simplest form the expression is \(7x + 17\). The constant term is the number standing alone with no \(x\), which is \(17\). So the answer is \(\boxed{17}\).

review.x: Distribute each number across its own parentheses in \(6(n-2) + 4(5-3n)\), letting every sign ride along, then collect the like terms. In simplest form, read off the coefficient of \(n\) and type that single value, sign included.
hints: Distribute one chunk at a time. \(6(n - 2)\) becomes \(6n - 12\), and \(4(5 - 3n)\) becomes \(20 - 12n\). Notice the \(-3n\) keeps its minus sign when the \(4\) rides through.
Now gather the \(n\) terms together and the plain numbers together. The \(n\) terms are \(6n\) and \(-12n\). Combine them and the result is the coefficient you want.
review.sol: Distribute each factor across its parentheses, keeping every sign attached to its term. The first chunk gives \(6(n - 2) = 6n - 12\). The second chunk gives \(4(5 - 3n) = 20 - 12n\), where the \(4\) multiplies both the \(5\) and the \(-3n\). So far the expression reads \(6n - 12 + 20 - 12n\). Now collect like terms. The \(n\) terms are \(6n\) and \(-12n\), which combine to \(-6n\). The plain numbers are \(-12\) and \(20\), which combine to \(8\). The simplest form is \(-6n + 8\). The coefficient of \(n\) is the number multiplying \(n\), which is \(-6\). \(\boxed{-6}\)

review.x: In \(20 - 3(2x - 4)\), the \(-3\) distributes across the group and flips the sign of every piece inside because it is negative. Simplify into a number plus a multiple of \(x\), then find the constant term, the plain number with no \(x\), and type that single value.
hints: Distribute the \(-3\) across both terms inside the group. Watch the signs carefully. You get \(-3 \times 2x = -6x\) and \(-3 \times (-4) = +12\), since a negative times a negative is positive.
After distributing you have \(20 - 6x + 12\). The only like terms are the two plain numbers \(20\) and \(12\). Combine just those, and whatever number is left standing alone is the constant term.
review.sol: Start by distributing the \(-3\) into the group \(2x - 4\). Multiply it by each term inside. You get \(-3 \times 2x = -6x\) and \(-3 \times (-4) = +12\). The negative in front flips the sign of the \(-4\), turning it into \(+12\). So the expression becomes \(20 - 6x + 12\). Now combine the like terms. The two plain numbers \(20\) and \(12\) join together into \(32\), while the \(-6x\) has no partner and stays as it is. The simplest form is \(32 - 6x\). The constant term is the number standing alone with no \(x\) attached, which is \(32\). So the value you read off is \(\boxed{32}\).

review.x: Two expressions are equivalent when their difference \(A - B\) is \(0\) for every value of \(x\). Let \(A = 3(2x + 5)\) and \(B = 6x + 15\). Evaluate \(A - B\) at \(x = 4\) by replacing every \(x\) with \(4\), and type the single number you get.
hints: First open up \(A\). The \(3\) outside multiplies both pieces inside, so \(3(2x + 5)\) becomes \(6x + 15\). Now compare that to \(B = 6x + 15\).
Subtract carefully. \(A - B = (6x + 15) - (6x + 15)\). Line up the \(6x\) with the \(6x\) and the \(15\) with the \(15\), then see what is left before you even reach for \(x = 4\).
review.sol: Start by simplifying \(A\). The \(3\) in front of \(3(2x + 5)\) gets shared with both terms inside, so \(3 \cdot 2x = 6x\) and \(3 \cdot 5 = 15\), which gives \(A = 6x + 15\). That is exactly the same expression as \(B = 6x + 15\). Now form the difference and combine like terms, \(A - B = (6x + 15) - (6x + 15) = 0\). The variable has already vanished, so the simplified difference is just \(0\). Plugging in \(x = 4\) cannot change a plain \(0\), and you can check it directly, since \(A = 3(2 \cdot 4 + 5) = 3 \cdot 13 = 39\) and \(B = 6 \cdot 4 + 15 = 24 + 15 = 39\), so \(A - B = 39 - 39 = 0\). The two builds agree, which is the whole point. The answer is \(\boxed{0}\).

review.x: The target is \(8x + 12\). Simplify each of \(4(2x + 3)\), \(2(4x + 6)\), \(8x + 6\), and \(12 + 8x\), then compare with the target. How many of the four are equivalent to \(8x + 12\)? Type the single count.
hints: Two expressions are equivalent when they give the same value for every choice of \(x\). To check, simplify each one by distributing any number sitting outside a set of parentheses, then line it up against \(8x + 12\).

Distributing means multiplying the outside number by each term inside, so \(4(2x + 3)\) becomes \(8x + 12\). Watch the third one carefully, the constant is \(6\) and not \(12\), and remember that \(12 + 8x\) is the same as \(8x + 12\) with the parts just written in the other order.
review.sol: Simplify each expression and compare it to the target \(8x + 12\). For the first, \(4(2x + 3) = 8x + 12\), which matches. For the second, \(2(4x + 6) = 8x + 12\), which also matches. For the third, \(8x + 6\) is already simplified, and its constant is \(6\) instead of \(12\), so it does not match. For the fourth, \(12 + 8x\) is the same two terms as \(8x + 12\) written in the other order, and order does not change the value, so it matches. Three of the four are equivalent to the target. \(\boxed{3}\)

review.x: Two dials add \(\tfrac{5x}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{x}{4}\) to a score, where \(x\) is the number of presses. Combine \(\tfrac{5x}{6} + \tfrac{x}{4}\) into one term using a common denominator of \(12\), keeping the \(x\). State the coefficient of \(x\) as a fraction in lowest terms, typed as a over b.
hints: A common denominator of \(12\) turns \(\tfrac{5x}{6}\) into \(\tfrac{10x}{12}\) and \(\tfrac{x}{4}\) into \(\tfrac{3x}{12}\). Now the two terms are ready to add.
Add the numerators over the shared \(12\) to get \(\tfrac{13x}{12}\). The coefficient of \(x\) is the fraction multiplying it, so it is \(\tfrac{13}{12}\), already in lowest terms.
review.sol: Rewrite each fraction with denominator \(12\). Since \(\tfrac{5x}{6} = \tfrac{10x}{12}\) and \(\tfrac{x}{4} = \tfrac{3x}{12}\), the sum becomes $$\frac{10x}{12} + \frac{3x}{12} = \frac{13x}{12}.$$ The combined term is \(\tfrac{13x}{12}\). The coefficient of \(x\) is the number multiplying it, which is \(\tfrac{13}{12}\). Since \(13\) is prime and does not divide \(12\), this fraction is already in lowest terms. The coefficient is \(\boxed{13/12}\).

review.x: A tiled border uses \(3(2s + 3)\) tiles in one row, \(2(s - 1)\) in another, plus \(5\) corner tiles, where \(s\) is a size setting. Build one expression for the total, simplify it, then evaluate at \(s = 4\) by replacing every \(s\) with \(4\). Type the single number you get.
hints: First expand each piece. \(3(2s + 3)\) becomes \(6s + 9\), and \(2(s - 1)\) becomes \(2s - 2\). Now add all the tiles together, including the \(5\) corner tiles.
Collect the \(s\) terms and the plain numbers separately. The \(s\) terms are \(6s + 2s = 8s\), and the numbers are \(9 - 2 + 5 = 12\), so the total is \(8s + 12\). Put \(s = 4\) into that.
review.sol: Start by expanding each grouped piece. The first row is \(3(2s + 3) = 6s + 9\), and the second row is \(2(s - 1) = 2s - 2\). Adding everything, including the \(5\) corner tiles, gives \(6s + 9 + 2s - 2 + 5\). Combine the like terms. The \(s\) terms give \(6s + 2s = 8s\), and the plain numbers give \(9 - 2 + 5 = 12\), so the simplified total is \(8s + 12\). Now evaluate at \(s = 4\) by replacing \(s\) with \(4\), which gives \(8 \cdot 4 + 12 = 32 + 12 = 44\). \(\boxed{44}\)

blocks.x: An expression like \(3x + 5\) just sits there describing a whole family of values, one for each \(x\). Set an equals sign between two expressions, like \(3x + 5 = 20\), and you make a claim that both sides come out to the same number. That claim is an equation, and the value of the variable that makes it true is a solution. Picture a two-pan balance with the equals sign as the level beam, and the variable as a gift in nested boxes you will unwrap.

A vending machine charges \(3\) tokens to wake up plus \(5\) tokens per snack, so \(w\) snacks cost \(5w + 3\). One kid spent exactly \(23\) tokens, giving \(5w + 3 = 23\). With no method yet, test whole numbers: at \(w = 3\) the left side is \(18\), at \(w = 5\) it is \(28\), so the answer sits between. The value that makes both sides equal is the solution. What whole number \(w\) makes \(5w + 3 = 23\)? Type that value.
hints: You already passed \(23\) between \(w=3\) (which gives \(18\)) and \(w=5\) (which gives \(28\)). The answer is the whole number sitting right between them, so test the one you skipped.
Try \(w=4\). Then \(5w\) is \(5\times 4=20\), and \(20+3=23\). That hits the target exactly, so that is your solution.
blocks.sol: We want the value of \(w\) that makes \(5w+3=23\) a true statement. Testing values is a fine way in, and the pattern points straight at it. At \(w=3\) the left side is \(18\), and at \(w=5\) it is \(28\), so the value that lands on \(23\) sits between them. Test the whole number in the gap. With \(w=4\) we get \(5\times 4=20\), and \(20+3=23\). The left side equals the right side, so \(w=4\) works. Substitution check, put \(w=4\) back into the original equation. The left side is \(5\times 4+3=20+3=23\), and the right side is \(23\), so both sides match and \(w=4\) really is the solution. \(\boxed{4}\)

blocks.x: Before solving an equation you should be able to check one. Substituting means replacing every \(x\) with a candidate and seeing whether both sides match. A note claims \(x = 7\) solves \(3x - 5 = 19\), but \(3(7) - 5 = 16 \neq 19\), so \(7\) fails. One of the values \(7\) or \(8\) actually solves \(3x - 5 = 19\). Type the value that works.
hints: A correct solution must make the left side, \(3x - 5\), come out to exactly \(19\). You already saw \(7\) gives \(16\), which is too small, so test the other candidate.
Substitute \(x = 8\). The left side becomes \(3(8) - 5\). Work out \(3 \times 8 = 24\), then \(24 - 5\), and see whether you land on \(19\).
blocks.sol: Checking a value means substituting it into the left side and seeing if you get \(19\). Test the note's claim first. With \(x = 7\), the left side is \(3(7) - 5 = 21 - 5 = 16\). Since \(16 \neq 19\), the candidate \(7\) fails and is not the solution. Now test the other candidate. With \(x = 8\), the left side is \(3(8) - 5 = 24 - 5 = 19\). That matches the right side exactly, \(19 = 19\), so both sides agree and \(8\) is a genuine solution. The value that solves \(3x - 5 = 19\) is \(\boxed{8}\).

blocks.x: Solution and checking. An equals sign claims the two sides are the same number, and a solution is the value of the variable that makes the claim true. The identities from 6.1 held for every value, but an equation like \(2x + 5 = 11\) is choosy and usually has exactly one. You never take an answer on faith: drop the value back into both sides and watch whether the beam hangs level. If both land on the same number it is a solution; if not, it is not. Checking is the proof the solving worked.
The equals sign was invented by Robert Recorde in 1557. He chose two parallel lines because nothing could be more equal. The symbol took about a century to become standard.

A thermos starts at \(t\) degrees, the afternoon adds \(17\), and the display settles at \(9\), so \(t + 17 = 9\). The variable \(t\) is sealed inside an add-\(17\) box. Lift those \(17\) off the left pan, and lift the same \(17\) off the right pan to keep the scale level. Solve \(t + 17 = 9\) and type the value of \(t\).
hints: The \(17\) is added to \(t\). To undo an addition and free the variable, do the opposite operation to both sides at once. What single move strips that \(+17\) off the left pan?
Subtract \(17\) from both sides. The left pan becomes just \(t\), and the right pan becomes \(9 - 17\). That difference dips below zero, which is fine since the thermos started cold.
blocks.sol: The variable \(t\) sits on the left, locked inside a \(+17\). To isolate it, undo that addition by subtracting \(17\) from both sides so the scale stays balanced. $$t + 17 = 9$$ $$t + 17 - 17 = 9 - 17$$ $$t = -8$$ Now check by putting \(-8\) back into the original equation. The left side becomes \(-8 + 17 = 9\), which matches the right side, so the statement is true. The value of \(t\) is \(\boxed{-8}\).

blocks.x: Six identical sandbags, each of unknown mass \(m\), together tip a balance to \(15\) units, so \(6m = 15\). The only thing wrapped around \(m\) is a times-\(6\), and division undoes multiplication, so divide both sides by \(6\) to leave one bag alone. The answer need not be a whole number, which is fine. Solve \(6m = 15\) and type \(m\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The variable is multiplied by \(6\), so to peel that away you do the opposite of multiplying. Divide both sides of the equation by \(6\).
Dividing both sides by \(6\) gives \(m = \frac{15}{6}\). Now reduce that fraction. Both \(15\) and \(6\) share a factor of \(3\), so cancel it to reach lowest terms.
blocks.sol: The variable \(m\) is multiplied by \(6\), so undo that by dividing both sides by \(6\). $$6m = 15$$ $$\frac{6m}{6} = \frac{15}{6}$$ $$m = \frac{15}{6}$$ The left side leaves \(m\) alone, and the right side is \(\frac{15}{6}\), which is not yet in lowest terms. Both \(15\) and \(6\) are divisible by \(3\), so \(\frac{15}{6} = \frac{15 \div 3}{6 \div 3} = \frac{5}{2}\). Check it by putting \(\frac{5}{2}\) back into the original equation. Then \(6m = 6 \cdot \frac{5}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15\), which matches the right side, so the balance holds. Each sandbag has mass \(\frac{5}{2}\) units. $$\boxed{5/2}$$

blocks.x: The balance rule. Do the exact same operation to both sides and the beam stays level, so the value that made the equation true stays true. Adding, subtracting, multiplying, or dividing both sides by the same nonzero amount only repackages the equation, never changing the answer. The one forbidden move is touching one side and leaving the other alone, since that secretly tips the scale and tells a lie. That is the whole permission slip for solving.

blocks.cap: Removing the same weight from both pans keeps the scale level. That is exactly why subtracting \(3\) from both sides of \(x + 3 = 8\) leaves \(x = 5\), with the equation still true. The block \(x\) never moved, so whatever it weighs has not changed. The balance just shows it plainly, \(x = 5\).

blocks.x: In \(4x - 9 = 7\), the variable wears two boxes: it was multiplied by \(4\) first, then had \(9\) taken away. To unwrap, peel in reverse and pull the outer box off first, so add \(9\) to both sides, then divide by \(4\). Solve \(4x - 9 = 7\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: The outer layer is the minus \(9\), so deal with it first. What can you add to both sides to undo subtracting \(9\)?
Add \(9\) to both sides to get \(4x = 16\). Now the variable wears only the times-\(4\) box, so divide both sides by \(4\).
blocks.sol: Peel the outer box first by adding \(9\) to both sides. $$4x - 9 + 9 = 7 + 9$$ The left side becomes \(4x\) and the right side becomes \(16\), so \(4x = 16\). Now the variable wears only the times-\(4\) box, so divide both sides by \(4\). $$\frac{4x}{4} = \frac{16}{4}$$ This gives \(x = 4\). Check by substituting back into the original equation. \(4(4) - 9 = 16 - 9 = 7\), which matches the right side, so it holds. The value of \(x\) is \(\boxed{4}\).

blocks.x: Start with \(n\), multiply by \(-3\), then add \(2\), and you reach \(10\), so \(-3n + 2 = 10\). The times-\(-3\) is the inner box and the add-\(2\) is the outer box, so peel from the outside in: subtract \(2\) first, then divide by \(-3\). Watch the signs, because the answer will not be a whole number. Solve \(-3n + 2 = 10\) and type \(n\) as a fraction in lowest terms, including its sign.
hints: The outer box is the \(+2\), since the \(2\) was added last. Strip it off first by subtracting \(2\) from both sides, and see what is left on each side.

After subtracting, you have \(-3n = 8\). The inner box is times \(-3\), so undo it by dividing both sides by \(-3\). A positive divided by a negative is negative, and \(8\) and \(3\) share no common factor, so it stays as a fraction.
blocks.sol: Peel the outer box first. Subtract \(2\) from both sides. $$-3n + 2 - 2 = 10 - 2$$ The left side collapses to \(-3n\) and the right side becomes \(8\), so \(-3n = 8\). Now undo the inner box by dividing both sides by \(-3\). $$\frac{-3n}{-3} = \frac{8}{-3}$$ On the left the \(-3\) cancels and leaves \(n\). On the right, a positive over a negative is negative, and \(8\) and \(3\) have no common factor, so it does not reduce. That gives \(n = -\tfrac{8}{3}\). A negative fraction is a perfectly good solution, the equation does not mind. Check it by substituting back. With \(n = -\tfrac{8}{3}\), the term \(-3n\) is \(-3 \cdot \left(-\tfrac{8}{3}\right) = 8\), and \(8 + 2 = 10\), which matches the right side. The solution is \(\boxed{-8/3}\).

blocks.x: Peel from the outside in. To free a variable, undo the operations stacked around it in reverse order, the last thing done first, like taking off your coat before your shirt. A constant added on the outside comes off before the number multiplying on the inside, and each peel is the opposite operation done to both sides at once, so the equation stays balanced and the answer never moves. When the variable stands bare, plug your number back in to be sure both sides agree.

Real equations are not always neat. Sometimes the variable's side is piled with several terms, like \(2x + 3x\), or hides a group waiting to be handed out, like \(4(t - 7)\). Before peeling a single layer, tidy that side first with the housekeeping from 6.1, gathering like terms and distributing groups. In every equation here the variable stays on one side and the other side stays a plain constant.

A delivery rider's day reads \(5k + 2 + 3k - 7 = 19\), where \(k\) is the number of trips. Do not undo anything yet. First tidy the cluttered left side by combining like terms, exactly as in 6.1, then isolate \(k\). Solve \(5k + 2 + 3k - 7 = 19\) and type the value of \(k\).
hints: Resist undoing anything first. The left side is a pile of like terms, so combine them before you touch the right side. Add up the \(k\) terms, \(5k\) and \(3k\), and separately add up the plain numbers, \(2\) and \(-7\).
After combining you get \(8k - 5 = 19\), a clean two-step. Add \(5\) to both sides to get \(8k = 24\), then divide both sides by \(8\).
blocks.sol: Tidy the left pan first by combining like terms, exactly as in 6.1. The \(k\) terms gather as \(5k + 3k = 8k\), and the plain numbers gather as \(2 - 7 = -5\), so the whole left side becomes \(8k - 5\). Notice the variable lived only on the left the whole time, so now the equation is a clean two-step. $$8k - 5 = 19$$ Add \(5\) to both sides to peel off the outer number. $$8k = 24$$ Divide both sides by \(8\). $$k = 3$$ Check by substituting \(k = 3\) back into the original left side, \(5(3) + 2 + 3(3) - 7 = 15 + 2 + 9 - 7 = 19\), which matches the right side, so the scale truly balances. The rider made \(\boxed{3}\) trips.

blocks.cap: Undo from the outside in. \(4x-9=7\) was built by \(\times 4\) then \(-9\); peel the last layer first \u2014 add 9 to get \(4x=16\), then divide by 4 to get \(x=4\). Check: \(4\cdot4-9=7\).

blocks.x: A side can hide its real shape inside a group. In \(6(2 - q) = 30\), the outer times-\(6\) has to reach both pieces inside before anything else can happen, so it meets the \(2\) and the \(-q\), and that minus sign rides along. Distribute carefully, then peel the two-step equation. Solve \(6(2 - q) = 30\) and type the value of \(q\).
hints: Hand the outer \(6\) to both pieces inside the group before you do anything else. The \(6\) hits the \(2\) and it also hits the \(-q\), so watch that the sign rides along. The left side turns into \(12 - 6q\).
Now you have \(12 - 6q = 30\), a plain two-step. Subtract \(12\) from both sides to get \(-6q = 18\), then divide both sides by \(-6\). A positive divided by a negative is negative.
blocks.sol: Start by distributing the outer \(6\) across the group, giving it to both the \(2\) and the \(-q\). $$6(2 - q) = 30$$ $$12 - 6q = 30$$ Notice the \(-q\) became \(-6q\), the minus sign came along for the ride. Now it is a clean two-step. Subtract \(12\) from both sides to strip the constant off the variable side. $$-6q = 18$$ Then divide both sides by \(-6\). A positive over a negative is negative. $$q = \frac{18}{-6} = -3$$ Check it by putting \(-3\) back into the original. Inside the group, \(2 - (-3) = 5\), and \(6 \times 5 = 30\), which matches the right side, so the equation holds. The value of \(q\) is \(\boxed{-3}\).

blocks.x: Simplify before peeling. Before touching both sides, clean up the side holding the variable: distribute any group with a number out front and gather every like term, until that side is as short as it goes, ideally one variable term and one plain number. A pan piled with loose terms or a sealed group is almost always a plain two-step once tidied. So the real first step of solving is the simplifying from 6.1 doing its job again, and only then do you start peeling.

A paint mixer uses two thirds of a tank to fill today's order, so \(\frac{2}{3}x = 10\), where \(x\) is the tank's capacity in liters. The variable is wrapped in a multiply-by-\(\frac{2}{3}\) box, so multiply both sides by the reciprocal \(\frac{3}{2}\), which turns the \(\frac{2}{3}\) into a plain \(1\). Solve \(\frac{2}{3}x = 10\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: The variable is being multiplied by \(\frac{2}{3}\). To undo a multiplication you do the opposite. Multiplying both sides by the reciprocal \(\frac{3}{2}\) will flip the \(\frac{2}{3}\) back to \(1\) and leave \(x\) alone on the left.
Multiply both sides by \(\frac{3}{2}\). The left side becomes \(x\). The right side becomes \(10 \cdot \frac{3}{2}\), so work out \(\frac{30}{2}\).
blocks.sol:
The variable is multiplied by \(\frac{2}{3}\), so multiply both sides by the reciprocal \(\frac{3}{2}\) to undo it.
$$\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3}x = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 10$$
On the left, \(\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 1\), so the left side is just \(x\). On the right, \(10 \cdot \frac{3}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15\).
$$x = 15$$
If you prefer, the same arithmetic comes from multiplying both sides by \(3\) to get \(2x = 30\), then dividing by \(2\) to get \(x = 15\). Either route lands in the same place. Check it by substituting \(x = 15\) back in. The left side is \(\frac{2}{3} \cdot 15 = \frac{30}{3} = 10\), which matches the right side, so the tank holds \(\boxed{15}\) liters.

blocks.cap: The bar under \(x+4\) means the whole group is split into \(5\) equal slices, so the equation reads \(\frac{x+4}{5}=3\). Multiplying both sides by that denominator \(5\) hands the five slices back as one whole group and undoes the division, turning the equation into the clean \(x+4=15\), since \(3\times 5=15\). From there it is one more step, peel off the \(+4\) from both sides, and the variable stands alone at \(x=11\).

blocks.x: Clear denominators first. When the variable is trapped under a fraction bar, the denominator is the outermost box, so make it disappear by multiplying both sides by it; the division cancels and you are left with whole-number pieces. This works whether the denominator wraps a whole side, like \(\frac{x+5}{4} = 3\), or appears as a fractional coefficient, like \(\frac{2}{3}x = 8\), where multiplying by the flipped fraction does the same job. It is still a fair both-sides move, so the answer never changes.

Solving for the variable is often just the first half of a question. A ticket booth's group passes \(x\) satisfy \(5(x - 2) = 35\), but the manager wants the wristband count, given by \(2x + 1\). First solve \(5(x - 2) = 35\) for \(x\), then compute \(2x + 1\). Type that final number.
hints: Start by solving for \(x\) on its own. The whole left side is multiplied by \(5\), so undo that first by dividing both sides by \(5\). Set the value of \(x\) aside, because the question is really asking about \(2x+1\).
Dividing both sides by \(5\) gives \(x-2=7\), so adding \(2\) to both sides gives \(x=9\). Now substitute into the wristband rule. Work out \(2\times 9+1\) and type that single number.
blocks.sol:
First solve the equation for \(x\). The left side has the whole group \(x-2\) multiplied by \(5\), so divide both sides by \(5\) to peel that off.
$$5(x-2)=35$$
$$x-2=7$$
Now add \(2\) to both sides to get \(x\) by itself.
$$x=9$$
Check this in the original equation by substituting \(x=9\). The left side becomes \(5(9-2)=5\times 7=35\), which matches the right side, so \(x=9\) is correct. But the question does not want \(x\). It wants the number of wristbands, which the rule writes as \(2x+1\). Substitute \(x=9\) into that expression.
$$2x+1=2\times 9+1=18+1=19$$
So the booth hands out \(19\) wristbands. The final value is \(\boxed{19}\).

blocks.x: Sometimes you are handed the variable and must hunt for a missing number. A game designer's win happens when a score \(x\) satisfies \(3x + c = 20\), where \(c\) is a fixed bonus she still has to choose. She wants \(x = 4\) to count as a win, which means \(x = 4\) must be a solution. Substitute \(4\) for \(x\), then solve for \(c\). Type the value of \(c\).
hints: You know what \(x\) is this time, so put it to work. A solution is a value that makes the equation true, so replace \(x\) with \(4\) in \(3x + c = 20\) and see what the equation turns into.
After substituting you get \(12 + c = 20\). Now \(c\) is the only unknown left, sitting on one side. Undo the \(+12\) by subtracting \(12\) from both sides.
blocks.sol: Saying \(x = 4\) is a solution means \(x = 4\) must make \(3x + c = 20\) a true statement. So substitute \(4\) in for \(x\). $$3(4) + c = 20$$ Multiply first to clean up the left side. $$12 + c = 20$$ Now \(c\) is alone on one side, and you isolate it by subtracting \(12\) from both sides. $$12 + c - 12 = 20 - 12$$ $$c = 8$$ Check it by putting both \(x = 4\) and \(c = 8\) back into the original rule. The left side is \(3(4) + 8 = 12 + 8 = 20\), and the right side is \(20\), so both sides land on \(20\) and the win condition holds. The fixed bonus the designer needs is \(\boxed{8}\).

blocks.x: Always check. Checking is the second half of solving, not a chore tacked on. Put the value you found back into the original equation, the messy one before you cleared or combined anything, and work out each side on its own. If both sides land on the same number, the equation is balanced and you know you are right. If they do not match, you have caught yourself, almost always a dropped negative or a small slip, before anyone else saw it.

A community garden shares a total across \(4\) equal plots, ending with \(6\) beds each, so \(\dfrac{3(m-2)+2m}{4} = 6\), where \(m\) is beds per row. Peel the outermost box first by multiplying both sides by \(4\), then distribute, combine like terms, and isolate \(m\). Solve \(\dfrac{3(m-2)+2m}{4} = 6\) and type the value of \(m\).
hints: The whole left side is wrapped in a division by 4. That is the outermost box, so undo it first. Multiply both sides by 4 to clear the denominator, and the equation becomes \(3(m-2)+2m=24\).

Now distribute the 3 across \(m-2\) to get \(3m-6\), then add the \(2m\). The like terms \(3m\) and \(2m\) combine into \(5m\), so the left collapses to \(5m-6=24\). Add 6 to both sides, then divide both sides by 5.
blocks.sol: Start with the equation and peel off the outermost box first. The entire left side is divided by 4, so undo that by multiplying both sides by 4. $$\frac{3(m-2)+2m}{4}=6$$ $$3(m-2)+2m=24$$ Now work inside. Distribute the 3 across \(m-2\) to get \(3m-6\), then bring down the \(2m\). $$3m-6+2m=24$$ Combine the like terms \(3m\) and \(2m\) into \(5m\), so the left side collapses. $$5m-6=24$$ Keep peeling from the outside. The \(-6\) is the last thing added on, so add 6 to both sides. $$5m=30$$ Finally \(m\) is multiplied by 5, so divide both sides by 5. $$m=6$$ The variable rode along on one side the whole way. Check by substituting \(m=6\) back into the original. The top becomes \(3(6-2)+2(6)=3\cdot 4+12=12+12=24\), and \(24\div 4=6\), which matches the right side. $$\boxed{6}$$

review.x: A diver's instrument reads higher as she rises, and her start reading \(x\) satisfies \(x + 12 = 5\), where negative values mean below the surface. Solve \(x + 12 = 5\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: The variable \(x\) is being added to by \(12\). To undo adding \(12\), do the opposite to both sides and subtract \(12\).
After you subtract \(12\) from the right side, you are computing \(5 - 12\), which lands below zero.
review.sol:
The variable \(x\) sits alone on the left, with \(12\) added to it, so I undo that addition. Subtract \(12\) from both sides to keep the equation balanced.

$$x + 12 = 5$$
$$x + 12 - 12 = 5 - 12$$
$$x = -7$$

So \(x = \boxed{-7}\). Checking it, substitute \(-7\) back into the left side. That gives \(-7 + 12 = 5\), which matches the right side, so the reading is correct.

review.x: A jar holds some marbles, and after \(6\) more are dropped in it holds \(20\). With \(n\) the starting count, that is \(n + 6 = 20\). Solve for \(n\) and type the value.
hints: The \(6\) is being added to \(n\), so undo it by subtracting \(6\) from both sides at once.
Subtracting \(6\) from the left leaves \(n\) all by itself, and subtracting \(6\) from the right turns \(20\) into \(20 - 6\).
review.sol: The variable \(n\) sits on the left with a \(6\) added to it, so to get \(n\) by itself, undo that addition by subtracting \(6\) from both sides. $$n + 6 = 20$$ $$n + 6 - 6 = 20 - 6$$ $$n = 14$$ So the jar started with \(\boxed{14}\) marbles. Checking it, \(14 + 6 = 20\), which matches the right side, so the value works.

review.x: Four identical packs together hold \(9\) mini sets, so \(4m = 9\), where \(m\) is how many fit in one pack. Solve for \(m\) and type the value as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Right now \(m\) is multiplied by 4. To get \(m\) by itself, do the opposite of multiplying and divide both sides by 4.
Dividing both sides by 4 gives \(m = \tfrac{9}{4}\). Check that 9 and 4 share no common factor, so it is already in lowest terms.
review.sol: We start with \(4m = 9\). Since \(m\) is multiplied by 4, we undo that by dividing both sides by 4. That gives \(m = \tfrac{9}{4}\). To check, put it back in and see if \(4 \cdot \tfrac{9}{4} = 9\), and it does. Since 9 and 4 have no common factor other than 1, the fraction is already in lowest terms, so \(\boxed{9/4}\).

review.x: A descending drone's tracker satisfies \(-6y = 54\). Divide both sides by \(-6\) to solve. Type the value of \(y\), including its sign.
hints: The \(y\) is being multiplied by \(-6\), so to get \(y\) by itself, divide both sides of the equation by \(-6\).
Work out the right side. You need \(54\) divided by \(-6\), which is \(-9\).
review.sol: We start with \(-6y = 54\). The \(y\) is multiplied by \(-6\), so we undo that by dividing both sides by \(-6\). On the left that leaves just \(y\). On the right we compute \(54\) divided by \(-6\). A positive divided by a negative is negative, and \(54\) divided by \(6\) is \(9\), so the result is \(-9\). That gives \(y = -9\). Check it by putting it back in. \(-6\) times \(-9\) is \(54\), which matches the right side, so the answer is \(\boxed{-9}\).

review.x: A vending machine charges \(x\) per snack plus a flat \(\$7\) fee, and \(3\) snacks come to \(\$22\), so \(3x + 7 = 22\). Peel the constant, then the coefficient. Solve and type the value of \(x\).
hints: The \(7\) is just added on at the end, so peel it off first. Subtract \(7\) from both sides to get \(3x\) by itself.
Now you have \(3x = 15\). Since \(3x\) means \(3\) times \(x\), divide both sides by \(3\) to find \(x\).
review.sol: Start with the equation and undo the operations in reverse, peeling off the constant first and then the coefficient. Subtract \(7\) from both sides to clear the added constant. $$3x + 7 - 7 = 22 - 7$$ $$3x = 15$$ Now \(3x\) means \(3\) times \(x\), so divide both sides by \(3\). $$\frac{3x}{3} = \frac{15}{3}$$ $$x = 5$$ Check by substituting \(x = 5\) back into the original equation. The left side becomes \(3(5) + 7 = 15 + 7 = 22\), which matches the right side, so the value works. \(\boxed{5}\)

review.x: A baker uses \(w\) cups on each of two trays and sets aside \(3\), using \(4\) cups in all, so \(2w - 3 = 4\). Add \(3\) to both sides, then divide by \(2\). Type \(w\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The lone number on the left is the \(-3\). Move it out of the way first by adding 3 to both sides, so the \(2w\) stands alone.
After adding 3 you get \(2w = 7\). Now undo the times-2 by dividing both sides by 2.
review.sol: Start with \(2w - 3 = 4\). The variable term has a number hanging off it, so peel that away first. Add 3 to both sides and the left becomes just \(2w\), while the right becomes \(4 + 3 = 7\). That leaves \(2w = 7\). Now \(w\) is only being multiplied by 2, so divide both sides by 2 to get \(w = \frac{7}{2}\). Check it by putting \(\frac{7}{2}\) back in. Then \(2 \cdot \frac{7}{2} = 7\), and \(7 - 3 = 4\), which matches the right side, so it holds. The dough on each tray is \(\boxed{7/2}\) cups.

review.x: Tidy the busy left side first, then hunt for the value. Solve \(7t - 2 - 4t + 5 = 18\) and type the value of \(t\).
hints: The left side has two \(t\) terms and two plain numbers. Combine them first. The \(t\) terms give \(7t - 4t = 3t\), and the numbers give \(-2 + 5 = 3\), so the equation becomes \(3t + 3 = 18\).
Now peel away the outside layers. Subtract \(3\) from both sides to undo the \(+3\), then divide both sides by \(3\) to undo the multiplication.
review.sol:
Start by cleaning up the left side, since the variable only lives over there. Group the \(t\) terms and the constants separately.
$$7t - 4t = 3t \quad\text{and}\quad -2 + 5 = 3$$
So the equation simplifies to a much friendlier shape.
$$3t + 3 = 18$$
Now isolate \(t\). Subtract \(3\) from both sides to clear the constant.
$$3t = 15$$
Then divide both sides by \(3\).
$$t = 5$$
Check it by putting \(5\) back into the original left side. We get \(7\cdot 5 - 2 - 4\cdot 5 + 5 = 35 - 2 - 20 + 5 = 18\), which matches the right side. So the solution is \(\boxed{5}\).

review.x: Five identical kits each ship with \(4\) items missing, so each holds \(d - 4\), and all five total \(15\), giving \(5(d - 4) = 15\). Distribute the \(5\) first, then isolate \(d\). Type the value of \(d\).
hints: Distribute the 5 across the parentheses. \(5(d-4)\) becomes \(5d-20\), so the equation reads \(5d-20=15\).
Now the variable is alone on the left as one term. Undo the \(-20\) by adding 20 to both sides, then undo the times 5 by dividing both sides by 5.
review.sol: Start by distributing the 5 across the parentheses so the left side is a clean sum. $$5(d-4)=15$$ $$5d-20=15$$ Now peel away the pieces around \(d\). Add 20 to both sides to clear the \(-20\). $$5d=35$$ Divide both sides by 5 to free \(d\). $$d=7$$ So \(\boxed{7}\). Check it by going back to the original equation. With \(d=7\) each kit holds \(7-4=3\) items, and five kits hold \(5\cdot 3 = 15\), which matches the 15 items counted, so the value works.

review.x: A triangle has three equal edges, each measuring \(2k + 5\), and its perimeter is \(9\), so \(3(2k + 5) = 9\). Distribute, then solve. Type the value of \(k\).
hints: Multiply the \(3\) through the parentheses first. \(3 \cdot 2k\) and \(3 \cdot 5\) give you \(6k+15=9\).
Now the variable is alone on the left once you peel away the constant. Subtract \(15\) from both sides, then divide both sides by \(6\).
review.sol: Start by distributing the \(3\) across the group. \(3(2k+5)\) becomes \(6k+15\), so the equation is now \(6k+15=9\). Undo the \(+15\) by subtracting \(15\) from both sides, which leaves \(6k=-6\). Then divide both sides by \(6\) to get \(k=-1\). $$3(2k+5)=9$$ $$6k+15=9$$ $$6k=-6$$ $$k=-1$$ Check it by putting \(-1\) back in. \(2(-1)+5=3\), and \(3 \cdot 3=9\), which matches the right side. So \(\boxed{-1}\).

review.x: Three fifths of a tank's capacity \(x\) comes to \(9\) liters, so \(\frac{3}{5}x = 9\). Multiply both sides by the reciprocal \(\frac{5}{3}\) to free \(x\). Type the value of \(x\).
hints: The variable is being multiplied by \(\frac{3}{5}\). To undo that, multiply both sides by the reciprocal, which is \(\frac{5}{3}\).
Compute \(9 \cdot \frac{5}{3}\). The \(9\) and the \(3\) share a factor, so this lands on a clean whole number.
review.sol:
The variable \(x\) is multiplied by \(\frac{3}{5}\), so I undo that by multiplying both sides by the reciprocal \(\frac{5}{3}\).
$$\frac{5}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{5}x = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 9$$
On the left the two fractions cancel to \(1\), leaving \(x\). On the right \(\frac{5}{3} \cdot 9 = \frac{45}{3} = 15\).
$$x = 15$$
Check by putting \(15\) back in. \(\frac{3}{5} \cdot 15 = \frac{45}{5} = 9\), which matches the right side, so it holds.
\(\boxed{15}\)

review.x: A fractional coefficient scales \(b\), and the other side is negative: \(\tfrac{2}{7}b = -4\). Free \(b\) by multiplying both sides by the flipped fraction \(\tfrac{7}{2}\), carrying the negative through. Type the value of \(b\).
hints: The coefficient \(\tfrac{2}{7}\) is multiplying \(b\), so undo it by multiplying both sides by its reciprocal \(\tfrac{7}{2}\). On the left, \(\tfrac{7}{2}\cdot\tfrac{2}{7} = 1\), which leaves a bare \(b\). On the right you still have to work out \(\tfrac{7}{2}\cdot(-4)\), and a positive times a negative stays negative.
Multiplying the right side, \(\tfrac{7}{2}\cdot(-4) = \tfrac{7\cdot(-4)}{2} = \tfrac{-28}{2} = -14\). So the whole equation collapses to \(b = -14\). Keep that minus sign, it does not disappear.
review.sol: The variable \(b\) is being multiplied by \(\tfrac{2}{7}\), so to isolate it I multiply both sides by the reciprocal \(\tfrac{7}{2}\). On the left those two fractions cancel to \(1\), and on the right I carry out the multiplication, keeping the sign. $$\tfrac{7}{2}\cdot\tfrac{2}{7}b = \tfrac{7}{2}\cdot(-4)$$ $$b = \tfrac{7\cdot(-4)}{2} = \tfrac{-28}{2} = -14$$ So \(b = -14\). Check it by substituting back into the original left side. \(\tfrac{2}{7}\cdot(-14) = \tfrac{2\cdot(-14)}{7} = \tfrac{-28}{7} = -4\), which matches the right side, so the value works. The solution is \(\boxed{-14}\).

review.x: A value \(3h + 2\) shared equally among \(4\) display cases gives \(5\) each, so \(\dfrac{3h+2}{4} = 5\). Undo the division first by multiplying both sides by \(4\), then solve. Type the value of \(h\).
hints: The left side is one big quantity divided by \(4\). Multiply both sides by \(4\) to clear the denominator, which turns the equation into \(3h+2=20\).
Now you have \(3h+2=20\). Peel the layers in reverse. Subtract \(2\) from both sides, then divide both sides by \(3\).
review.sol:
The variable sits inside a fraction, and the whole top is divided by \(4\). Undo that division first by multiplying both sides by \(4\).
$$\frac{3h+2}{4}=5$$
$$3h+2=20$$
Now the variable has two layers wrapped around it. A \(2\) is added, and the result is multiplied by \(3\). Peel them off in reverse order. Subtract \(2\) from both sides.
$$3h=18$$
Then divide both sides by \(3\).
$$h=6$$
Check by putting \(6\) back in. The top becomes \(3\cdot 6+2=20\), and \(\frac{20}{4}=5\), which matches the right side. So \(\boxed{6}\).

review.x: A quantity \(5x + 2\) divided into \(4\) equal parts gives \(6\) each, so \(\frac{5x+2}{4} = 6\). Clear the denominator first by multiplying both sides by \(4\), then solve. Type \(x\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The variable is trapped over a denominator of \(4\). Undo that division first by multiplying both sides by \(4\), which turns the left side into just \(5x+2\).
Once you have \(5x+2=24\), subtract \(2\) from both sides to get \(5x=22\), then divide both sides by \(5\). Since \(22\) is not a multiple of \(5\), your answer stays a fraction.
review.sol:
The variable sits over a denominator, so clear that first. Multiply both sides by \(4\).
$$\frac{5x+2}{4}\cdot 4 = 6\cdot 4$$
$$5x+2 = 24$$
Now subtract \(2\) from both sides.
$$5x = 22$$
Divide both sides by \(5\).
$$x = \frac{22}{5}$$
Since \(22\) and \(5\) share no common factor, this is already in lowest terms. Check it by putting \(\frac{22}{5}\) back in. Then \(5x = 5\cdot\frac{22}{5} = 22\), so \(5x+2 = 24\), and \(\frac{24}{4}=6\), which matches the right side. The answer is \(\boxed{22/5}\).

review.x: You are told \(n = 5\) is a solution of \(2n + c = 17\), so plugging in \(5\) must make it true. Substitute \(n = 5\), then solve for the constant \(c\). Type the value of \(c\).
hints: Replace \(n\) with 5 everywhere it shows up. The equation becomes \(2(5) + c = 17\), and \(2(5)\) is just 10.
Now you have \(10 + c = 17\). Ask what number added to 10 lands on 17, or subtract 10 from both sides.
review.sol:
A solution is a value of the variable that makes the equation true, so saying \(n = 5\) is a solution means \(2n + c = 17\) has to hold when we put 5 in for \(n\). Substitute first.
$$2(5) + c = 17$$
$$10 + c = 17$$
Now isolate \(c\) by subtracting 10 from both sides.
$$10 + c - 10 = 17 - 10$$
$$c = 7$$
Check it by going back to the original equation with \(n = 5\) and \(c = 7\). The left side is \(2(5) + 7 = 10 + 7 = 17\), which matches the right side, so the constant is \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.x: In 6.2 the unknown always sat on one side while plain numbers waited on the other. Real situations are not always so tidy, and the same unknown can show up on both sides at once. Picture two scooter apps: one costs \(2m + 5\) and the other \(3m\), so they match when \(2m + 5 = 3m\), with \(m\) on both pans. You cannot isolate the variable while copies sit on each side, so this lesson adds exactly one new move and everything else carries straight over.

A locker rental costs \(7x + 2\) and a bike rental costs \(4x + 17\), each over the same number of days \(x\), and today they came out equal, so \(7x + 2 = 4x + 17\). The new idea: the balance rule lets you take the same term off both pans, and that term can be a variable. Subtract \(4x\) from both sides to clear the \(x\) off the right, then solve the one-sided equation. Solve \(7x + 2 = 4x + 17\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: Both sides carry an \(x\) term. Subtract the smaller one, \(4x\), from each side so the variable survives on only one side.
After subtracting \(4x\) you get \(3x + 2 = 17\). Now it is an ordinary two-step equation, subtract 2 then divide by 3.
blocks.sol: The variable appears on both sides, so first gather it onto one side. Subtract \(4x\) from both sides. On the left \(7x - 4x = 3x\), and on the right the \(4x\) is gone, leaving \(3x + 2 = 17\). Now it is the familiar one-sided kind. Subtract 2 from both sides to get \(3x = 15\), then divide both sides by 3 to get \(x = 5\). Check by putting 5 back into the original. The left is \(7(5) + 2 = 37\) and the right is \(4(5) + 17 = 37\), a match, so \(\boxed{5}\).

blocks.x: Gather the variable to one side. When the unknown shows up on both sides, the one new move is to gather every variable term onto a single side. The balance principle still rules, so subtracting or adding the same variable term to both sides keeps the equation true, exactly as subtracting a number did. Once the variable lives on only one side, you are back to the one-sided equations from 6.2 and you finish the same way.
