blocks.cap: The equation \(4x = x + 6\) holds an \(x\) on each pan. Lifting one \(x\) off both pans at once keeps the scale level and clears the variable from the right, leaving \(3x = 6\). From there a single division gives \(x = 2\). Taking the same variable term off both sides is the one new move in this lesson.

blocks.x: When the bigger pile of variables is on the right, a little planning avoids negatives. In \(9 + 3x = 7x + 1\), subtracting \(7x\) would leave a negative \(x\) term, so instead subtract the smaller variable term, \(3x\), from both sides so the survivor stays positive. Solve \(9 + 3x = 7x + 1\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: Compare the two variable terms, \(3x\) and \(7x\). Subtract the smaller, \(3x\), from both sides to keep the remaining \(x\) term positive.
After subtracting \(3x\) you get \(9 = 4x + 1\). Subtract 1, then divide by 4.
blocks.sol: The variable terms are \(3x\) and \(7x\). Subtract the smaller, \(3x\), from both sides so the survivor stays positive. The left becomes \(9\) and the right becomes \(4x + 1\), giving \(9 = 4x + 1\). Subtract 1 from both sides to get \(8 = 4x\), then divide by 4 to get \(x = 2\). A quick check, the original left is \(9 + 3(2) = 15\) and the right is \(7(2) + 1 = 15\), so \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: Before moving anything across, tidy each side on its own. The left side of \(5x + 4 - 2x = x + 12\) has like terms hiding in it, so combine them first, then gather the variable onto one side. Solve \(5x + 4 - 2x = x + 12\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: The left side is not in simplest form. Combine \(5x\) and \(-2x\) into \(3x\), so the equation reads \(3x + 4 = x + 12\).
Now subtract \(x\) from both sides to get \(2x + 4 = 12\), then finish with the usual two steps.
blocks.sol: First simplify the left side by combining like terms, \(5x - 2x = 3x\), so the equation becomes \(3x + 4 = x + 12\). Now gather the variable. Subtract \(x\) from both sides to get \(2x + 4 = 12\). Subtract 4 to get \(2x = 8\), then divide by 2 to get \(x = 4\). Check, the original left is \(5(4) + 4 - 2(4) = 16\) and the right is \(4 + 12 = 16\), so \(\boxed{4}\).

blocks.x: A group can hide the variable until you distribute. In \(2(3x - 4) = 4x + 6\), expand the left side with the distributive property from 6.1, then collect the variable on one side. Solve \(2(3x - 4) = 4x + 6\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: Distribute the 2 across the group first. \(2(3x - 4)\) becomes \(6x - 8\), so the equation is \(6x - 8 = 4x + 6\).
Subtract \(4x\) from both sides to get \(2x - 8 = 6\), then add 8 and divide by 2.
blocks.sol: Distribute the 2 on the left, \(2(3x - 4) = 6x - 8\), so the equation becomes \(6x - 8 = 4x + 6\). Gather the variable by subtracting \(4x\) from both sides, leaving \(2x - 8 = 6\). Add 8 to both sides to get \(2x = 14\), then divide by 2 to get \(x = 7\). Check, the left is \(2(3 \cdot 7 - 4) = 2(17) = 34\) and the right is \(4(7) + 6 = 34\), so \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.x: The full recipe. First simplify each side on its own by distributing and combining like terms. Next gather all the variable terms onto one side and the plain numbers onto the other, moving the smaller variable term so the survivor stays positive. Then isolate the variable the way you did in 6.2, and finish by checking your answer in the original equation. Every linear equation in this course comes apart this way.
About 1,700 years ago Diophantus of Alexandria used shorthand symbols for unknowns. He discarded any puzzle whose answer came out negative, calling such results absurd.

Nothing says the meeting value has to be a whole number. Solve \(8x - 3 = 3x + 4\), gathering the variable onto one side, and let the answer be a fraction if that is where it lands. Type \(x\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Subtract \(3x\) from both sides to get \(5x - 3 = 4\).
Add 3 to get \(5x = 7\), then divide both sides by 5. The answer is a fraction, so leave it in lowest terms.
blocks.sol: Gather the variable by subtracting \(3x\) from both sides, which gives \(5x - 3 = 4\). Add 3 to both sides to get \(5x = 7\), then divide both sides by 5 to get \(x = \tfrac{7}{5}\). Since 7 and 5 share no common factor, it is already in lowest terms. Check, the left is \(8 \cdot \tfrac{7}{5} - 3 = \tfrac{56}{5} - \tfrac{15}{5} = \tfrac{41}{5}\) and the right is \(3 \cdot \tfrac{7}{5} + 4 = \tfrac{21}{5} + \tfrac{20}{5} = \tfrac{41}{5}\), so \(\boxed{7/5}\).

blocks.x: When a fraction sits in the equation, clear it first. In \(\dfrac{x}{3} + 4 = x - 2\), multiply every term on both sides by \(3\) to wipe out the denominator, then solve the both-sides equation that remains. Solve \(\dfrac{x}{3} + 4 = x - 2\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: Multiply every term on both sides by 3 to clear the denominator. \(\tfrac{x}{3}\) becomes \(x\), the 4 becomes 12, and the right side \(x - 2\) becomes \(3x - 6\).
Now solve \(x + 12 = 3x - 6\). Subtract \(x\), then finish.
blocks.sol: Clear the fraction by multiplying every term on both sides by 3. The left \(\tfrac{x}{3} + 4\) becomes \(x + 12\), and the right \(x - 2\) becomes \(3x - 6\), so the equation is \(x + 12 = 3x - 6\). Subtract \(x\) from both sides to get \(12 = 2x - 6\), add 6 to get \(18 = 2x\), then divide by 2 to get \(x = 9\). Check in the original, the left is \(\tfrac{9}{3} + 4 = 3 + 4 = 7\) and the right is \(9 - 2 = 7\), so \(\boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: Special cases. Once in a while the variable terms cancel completely when you gather them, leaving a statement with no variable at all. If that statement is true, like \(6 = 6\), then every number was a solution, so there are infinitely many. If it is false, like \(5 = -2\), then no number can ever work, so there is no solution. The leftover statement tells you which case you are in.
Try to solve \(6x + 5 = 6x - 2\). Gather the variable terms by subtracting \(6x\) from both sides and watch what remains. How many solutions does this equation have? Type the single number.
hints: Subtract \(6x\) from both sides and watch the variable disappear entirely.
You are left with \(5 = -2\), which is never true. An equation that reduces to a false statement has no solution, so the count is 0.
blocks.sol: Gather the variable by subtracting \(6x\) from both sides. Every \(x\) vanishes, and you are left with \(5 = -2\). That statement is false, and no value of \(x\) can ever make \(6x + 5\) equal \(6x - 2\), since the two sides always differ by 7. So the equation has no solution, a count of \(\boxed{0}\).

blocks.x: Now try \(3(2x + 4) = 6x + 12\). Distribute the left side, gather the variable, and notice what is left. To feel the result, test three values, \(x = 0\), \(x = 7\), and \(x = 20\). How many of those three are solutions? Type the single count.
hints: Distribute the 3 on the left. \(3(2x + 4)\) becomes \(6x + 12\), which is exactly the right side.
Since both sides are identical, every value of \(x\) is a solution, so all three test values work. Count them.
blocks.sol: Distribute the left side, \(3(2x + 4) = 6x + 12\), which is precisely the right side. Gathering the variable by subtracting \(6x\) leaves \(12 = 12\), a statement that is always true. That means every value of \(x\) is a solution, so the equation has infinitely many. In particular all three test values \(x = 0\), \(x = 7\), and \(x = 20\) work, which is \(\boxed{3}\) of the three. The equation is really just two ways of writing the same expression, the equivalent expressions you met in 6.1.

blocks.x: A shop compares two presses over \(t\) hours. The old press prints \(3(2t + 5)\) pages and the new one prints \(4t + 27\), and at one hour count they match. Distribute the left side, gather the variable, and find it. Solve \(3(2t + 5) = 4t + 27\) and type the value of \(t\).
hints: Distribute the 3 first. \(3(2t + 5)\) becomes \(6t + 15\), so the equation is \(6t + 15 = 4t + 27\).
Subtract \(4t\) from both sides to get \(2t + 15 = 27\), then subtract 15 and divide by 2.
blocks.sol: Distribute the left side, \(3(2t + 5) = 6t + 15\), so the equation is \(6t + 15 = 4t + 27\). Gather the variable by subtracting \(4t\) from both sides, leaving \(2t + 15 = 27\). Subtract 15 to get \(2t = 12\), then divide by 2 to get \(t = 6\). Check, the old press gives \(3(2 \cdot 6 + 5) = 3(17) = 51\) and the new gives \(4(6) + 27 = 51\), so \(\boxed{6}\).

review.x: Solve \(9x = 5x + 12\) and type the value of \(x\).
hints: Subtract \(5x\) from both sides to gather the variable on the left.
That leaves \(4x = 12\). Divide both sides by 4.
review.sol: Subtract \(5x\) from both sides, \(9x - 5x = 4x\), leaving \(4x = 12\). Divide both sides by 4 to get \(x = 3\). Check, the left is \(9(3) = 27\) and the right is \(5(3) + 12 = 27\), so \(\boxed{3}\).

review.x: Solve \(7y + 4 = 2y + 24\) and type the value of \(y\).
hints: Subtract \(2y\) from both sides to get \(5y + 4 = 24\).
Subtract 4, then divide by 5.
review.sol: Subtract \(2y\) from both sides to get \(5y + 4 = 24\). Subtract 4 to get \(5y = 20\), then divide by 5 to get \(y = 4\). Check, the left is \(7(4) + 4 = 32\) and the right is \(2(4) + 24 = 32\), so \(\boxed{4}\).

review.x: First combine like terms on the left, then gather the variable. Solve \(7t + 2 - 3t = t + 17\) and type the value of \(t\).
hints: Combine \(7t\) and \(-3t\) into \(4t\), so the equation reads \(4t + 2 = t + 17\).
Subtract \(t\) from both sides to get \(3t + 2 = 17\), then finish.
review.sol: Combine the like terms on the left, \(7t - 3t = 4t\), so the equation is \(4t + 2 = t + 17\). Subtract \(t\) from both sides to get \(3t + 2 = 17\), subtract 2 to get \(3t = 15\), then divide by 3 to get \(t = 5\). Check, the left is \(7(5) + 2 - 3(5) = 22\) and the right is \(5 + 17 = 22\), so \(\boxed{5}\).

review.x: Distribute the left side first, then gather the variable. Solve \(6(w - 2) = 3w + 6\) and type the value of \(w\).
hints: Distribute the 6. \(6(w - 2)\) becomes \(6w - 12\), so the equation is \(6w - 12 = 3w + 6\).
Subtract \(3w\) from both sides to get \(3w - 12 = 6\), then add 12 and divide by 3.
review.sol: Distribute the left side, \(6(w - 2) = 6w - 12\), so the equation is \(6w - 12 = 3w + 6\). Subtract \(3w\) from both sides to get \(3w - 12 = 6\). Add 12 to get \(3w = 18\), then divide by 3 to get \(w = 6\). Check, the left is \(6(6 - 2) = 24\) and the right is \(3(6) + 6 = 24\), so \(\boxed{6}\).

review.x: Solve \(7x + 1 = 2x + 9\) and type \(x\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Subtract \(2x\) from both sides to get \(5x + 1 = 9\).
Subtract 1 to get \(5x = 8\), then divide by 5. The answer is a fraction in lowest terms.
review.sol: Subtract \(2x\) from both sides to get \(5x + 1 = 9\). Subtract 1 to get \(5x = 8\), then divide by 5 to get \(x = \tfrac{8}{5}\). Since 8 and 5 share no common factor, it is in lowest terms. Check, the left is \(7 \cdot \tfrac{8}{5} + 1 = \tfrac{56}{5} + \tfrac{5}{5} = \tfrac{61}{5}\) and the right is \(2 \cdot \tfrac{8}{5} + 9 = \tfrac{16}{5} + \tfrac{45}{5} = \tfrac{61}{5}\), so \(\boxed{8/5}\).

review.x: Clear the denominator first by multiplying every term by \(2\). Solve \(\dfrac{y}{2} + 5 = y - 1\) and type the value of \(y\).
hints: Multiply every term on both sides by 2. \(\tfrac{y}{2}\) becomes \(y\), the 5 becomes 10, and the right side \(y - 1\) becomes \(2y - 2\).
Now solve \(y + 10 = 2y - 2\). Subtract \(y\), then finish.
review.sol: Multiply every term on both sides by 2 to clear the fraction. The left \(\tfrac{y}{2} + 5\) becomes \(y + 10\), and the right \(y - 1\) becomes \(2y - 2\), so the equation is \(y + 10 = 2y - 2\). Subtract \(y\) from both sides to get \(10 = y - 2\), then add 2 to get \(y = 12\). Check, the left is \(\tfrac{12}{2} + 5 = 6 + 5 = 11\) and the right is \(12 - 1 = 11\), so \(\boxed{12}\).

review.x: Gather the variable in \(3x - 4 = 3x + 5\) by subtracting \(3x\) from both sides, then read what remains. How many solutions does it have? Type the single number.
hints: Subtract \(3x\) from both sides and watch the variable vanish.
You are left with \(-4 = 5\), which is never true, so there is no solution. The count is 0.
review.sol: Subtract \(3x\) from both sides. The variable cancels completely and leaves \(-4 = 5\), a false statement. The two sides always differ by 9, so no value of \(x\) can ever make them equal. The equation has no solution, a count of \(\boxed{0}\).

review.x: Distribute the left side of \(4(x + 2) = 4x + 8\) and compare it with the right. Test \(x = 1\), \(x = 9\), and \(x = 50\). How many of the three are solutions? Type the count.
hints: Distribute the 4. \(4(x + 2)\) becomes \(4x + 8\), exactly the right side.
The two sides are identical, so every value of \(x\) works, including all three test values. Count them.
review.sol: Distribute the left side, \(4(x + 2) = 4x + 8\), which matches the right side exactly. Gathering the variable leaves \(8 = 8\), always true, so every value of \(x\) is a solution and there are infinitely many. All three test values \(x = 1\), \(x = 9\), and \(x = 50\) work, which is \(\boxed{3}\) of the three.

review.x: Two gyms charge by the month: over \(m\) months gym A costs \(7m + 18\) and gym B costs \(9m\). Find the month count where they cost the same. Solve \(7m + 18 = 9m\) and type the value of \(m\).
hints: Subtract \(7m\) from both sides to get \(18 = 2m\).
Divide both sides by 2.
review.sol: Subtract \(7m\) from both sides to gather the variable on the right, leaving \(18 = 2m\). Divide both sides by 2 to get \(m = 9\). Check, gym A costs \(7(9) + 18 = 81\) and gym B costs \(9(9) = 81\), so they match at \(\boxed{9}\) months.

blocks.x: Every word problem is one fact written twice, once in plain English and once in algebra, and the two versions say the identical thing. A sentence like a number tripled and then dropped by four lands on eleven is just \(3x - 4 = 11\) wearing a coat of words. Use the same five steps every time: read it all the way through, name the unknown with a letter, translate each phrase by its meaning, solve, then answer the exact question asked and check it against the original story.

An arcade prize machine doubles the bronze tokens you feed it and drops in \(7\) bonus tokens, and after Priya's turn the screen reads \(31\). In other words, seven more than twice her tokens is thirty-one. How many bronze tokens did Priya feed the machine?
hints: Give the unknown a name first. Let \(x\) be the number of tokens Priya fed the machine. Now translate the sentence one phrase at a time. What does "twice her tokens" become, and what does "seven more than" that become? The words "is thirty-one" hand you the equals sign.

Twice her tokens is \(2x\), and seven more than that is \(2x + 7\). The phrase "is thirty-one" sets it equal to \(31\), so your equation is \(2x + 7 = 31\). Subtract \(7\) from both sides, then divide by \(2\).
blocks.sol: Start by naming the unknown. Let \(x\) be the number of bronze tokens Priya fed the machine. Now build the equation by translating the words one piece at a time. "Twice her tokens" is \(2x\). "Seven more than" that means you add seven, giving \(2x + 7\). The phrase "is thirty-one" supplies the equals sign, so the equation is \(2x + 7 = 31\). Subtract \(7\) from both sides to get \(2x = 24\), then divide both sides by \(2\) to find \(x = 12\). The question asks how many tokens Priya fed the machine, which is exactly \(x\), so she fed in \(12\) tokens. Read it back against the words to check. Double \(12\) is \(24\), and seven more is \(31\), matching the screen. \(\boxed{12}\)

blocks.x: A beekeeper scoops out exactly a third of whatever a jar holds, pours it into a bottle, then stirs in \(9\) more ounces. The bottle then weighs \(17\) ounces, since here the word of means multiply. How many ounces of honey did the jar hold at the very start?
hints: Let \(x\) stand for the number of ounces the jar held at the start. Now read the words one piece at a time and turn each piece into math. "A third of the amount" becomes \(\tfrac{1}{3}x\), "increased by nine" adds \(9\), and "is seventeen" gives you the equals sign and the \(17\).
Your equation is \(\tfrac{1}{3}x + 9 = 17\). Subtract \(9\) from both sides to get \(\tfrac{1}{3}x = 8\), then multiply both sides by \(3\) to undo the one third.
blocks.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of ounces the jar held at the very start. Now translate the words piece by piece. A third of that amount is \(\tfrac{1}{3}x\), increasing it by nine ounces adds \(9\), and the words tell you this is seventeen, so the equation is \(\tfrac{1}{3}x + 9 = 17\). Subtract \(9\) from both sides to get \(\tfrac{1}{3}x = 8\). Multiply both sides by \(3\) to undo the one third, which gives \(x = 24\). Read it back against the words to check. A third of \(24\) is \(8\) ounces, and nine more ounces brings it to \(17\), which matches the scale. The question asks how many ounces the jar held at the start, so the answer is \(\boxed{24}\) ounces.

blocks.x: Maya tapes streamers to a wall, and the number on the wall is nineteen fewer than the number in her box, which counts out to \(40\). Watch the order: fewer than subtracts from the number, so this is \(x - 19\), not \(19 - x\). How many streamers were in Maya's box to start with?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number of streamers in the box. The phrase "nineteen fewer than the number" means you start with the number and take 19 away, so it becomes \(x - 19\), not \(19 - x\). Set that equal to 40.
Solve \(x - 19 = 40\) by adding 19 to both sides. Then read your answer back into the words to make sure nineteen fewer really lands on forty.
blocks.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of streamers in Maya's box to start with. The tricky phrase is "nineteen fewer than the number." Fewer than subtracts the nineteen from the number, and it reverses the order you hear, so it translates to \(x - 19\), not \(19 - x\). Since that equals forty, the equation is \(x - 19 = 40\). Add 19 to both sides to get \(x = 59\). Now check against the words. Nineteen fewer than 59 is \(59 - 19 = 40\), which matches the count on the wall. The wrong reading \(19 - x\) would have given \(19 - 59 = -40\), a negative number of streamers, which makes no sense, so the order really does flip. The question asks how many streamers were in the box to start, which is \(x\), so the box held \(\boxed{59}\) streamers.

blocks.x: The five steps. First name what the letter stands for and write that line down, so you do not solve for the wrong thing. Then translate the sentence into an equation one phrase at a time, by meaning rather than left to right, since a phrase like seven less than the number is \(x - 7\), not \(7 - x\). Solve with the tools you already have, report the exact quantity the question asked for, and finally read your answer back into the original sentence to be sure it fits.

A workshop has two number machines. The first triples a secret number and subtracts \(5\), and its readout shows \(40\). The second doubles that same number and adds \(2\). First solve for the secret number, then feed it into the second machine. What does the second machine's readout show?
hints: Give the secret number a name, say \(n\). The first machine triples it and subtracts five to read forty, so that sentence becomes an equation in \(n\). Solve that equation first, then look again at what the question is actually asking for.
Solve \(3n - 5 = 40\) to find \(n = 15\), but do not stop there. The question asks for the second machine's reading, which is \(2n + 2\). Put \(n = 15\) into \(2n + 2\) and compute that number.
blocks.sol: Let \(n\) be the secret number. The first machine triples \(n\) and subtracts five, and the readout shows forty, so the words become \(3n - 5 = 40\). Add five to both sides to get \(3n = 45\), then divide by three to get \(n = 15\). Here is the trap. It is tempting to type \(15\) and call it done, but \(15\) is the secret number, not the thing the question asked for. The question asks what the second machine reads. The second machine doubles the number and adds two, so its reading is \(2n + 2\). Substitute \(n = 15\) to get \(2 \cdot 15 + 2 = 30 + 2 = 32\). Read it back against the words. The first machine triples \(15\) to \(45\) and subtracts \(5\) to show \(40\), which matches. Feed \(15\) into the second machine, double it to \(30\), add \(2\), and the readout shows \(32\). The variable \(n\) was just a helper to find the secret number. The answer to the question asked is \(\boxed{32}\).

blocks.x: Two traps. First, words do not always run in the same order as the math. Phrases like less than, fewer than, and subtracted from flip the two pieces, so seven less than a number is \(x - 7\) while a number subtracted from seven is \(7 - x\). Read for meaning, not left to right. Second, the letter you pick is just a helper, and the question often asks for something else, so underline the actual question and translate your answer back at the end.

A rooftop garden has \(96\) plants, all marigolds and lavender, with three times as many lavender as marigolds. Let \(m\) be the number of marigolds, so the lavender count is \(3m\) and the two add to \(96\). The question asks for lavender, not \(m\). How many lavender plants are there?
hints: Pick the smaller, simpler quantity to name with a variable. Let \(m\) be the number of marigolds. Then write the lavender count in terms of \(m\), and remember the two kinds together make \(96\) plants.

There are three times as many lavender as marigolds, so the lavender count is \(3m\). The total ties them together, so \(m + 3m = 96\). Combine to get \(4m = 96\), solve for \(m\), then read the question again. It asks for lavender, which is \(3m\), not \(m\).
blocks.sol: Start by naming the quantity that everything else leans on. Let \(m\) be the number of marigolds. The garden has three times as many lavender plants as marigolds, so the lavender count is \(3m\). The two kinds are the whole garden, and the whole garden is \(96\) plants, so the marigolds plus the lavender give \(m + 3m = 96\). Combine the like terms to get \(4m = 96\), and divide both sides by \(4\) to find \(m = 24\). So there are \(24\) marigolds. Now reread the question. It does not ask for marigolds, it asks for lavender, which is \(3m\). That is \(3 \cdot 24 = 72\). The \(24\) was just the variable you chose along the way, not the thing being asked. Check it against the words. There are \(24\) marigolds and \(72\) lavender, and \(24 + 72 = 96\) plants in all, and \(72\) is indeed three times \(24\). The number of lavender plants is \(\boxed{72}\).

blocks.x: The variable is a tool, not always the prize. In the garden problem all the algebra was right and \(m\) came out to \(24\), but \(24\) was the marigold count, while the question asked for lavender, so the real answer was \(72\). Before you start, find the actual question and underline it. Once you have your number, ask whether it is what the question wanted, and if not, take the one more step to turn the variable into the quantity asked for.

A \(150\) cm ribbon is cut into two pieces, with the longer piece \(30\) cm more than the shorter. Let \(x\) be the shorter piece, so the longer is \(x + 30\) and the two add to \(150\). The question asks for the longer piece, not \(x\). How long is the longer piece, in centimeters?
hints: You have two unknown lengths, but you do not need two letters. Let \(x\) stand for the shorter piece. Since the longer piece is \(30\) more than the shorter one, you can write it as \(x + 30\). Now both pieces are written with the same single letter.

The two pieces together make the whole \(150\) centimeter ribbon, so \(x + (x + 30) = 150\). Combine to get \(2x + 30 = 150\), then \(2x = 120\), so \(x = 60\). That is the shorter piece. The question asks for the longer piece, which is \(x + 30\).
blocks.sol: There are two lengths to find, but tying them together lets one letter do all the work. Let \(x\) be the length of the shorter piece in centimeters. The longer piece is \(30\) centimeters longer than the shorter one, so it is \(x + 30\). Notice that the second unknown is now written in terms of the first, so the whole problem rides on a single variable. The two pieces came from one ribbon, so their lengths add to \(150\). That gives the equation $$x + (x + 30) = 150.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(2x + 30 = 150\). Subtract \(30\) from both sides to reach \(2x = 120\), then divide both sides by \(2\) to find \(x = 60\). So the shorter piece is \(60\) centimeters. The question did not ask for \(x\), it asked for the longer piece, which is \(x + 30 = 60 + 30 = 90\). Read it back against the words to check. The shorter piece is \(60\) and the longer is \(90\), they differ by \(30\), and \(60 + 90 = 150\), matching the full ribbon. The longer piece is \(\boxed{90}\) centimeters.

blocks.x: One variable for two unknowns. When two unknown amounts are locked together by a total you already know, you never need two letters. Call one of them \(x\), and since the pieces must add to the total, the other is just the total minus \(x\). Now everything is written with the one variable you know how to solve for, and the two-thing puzzle quietly becomes an ordinary one-variable equation.

blocks.cap: Most rows translate straight across, word by word in the same order. The one in gold is the one that catches people. Words like less than, fewer than, and subtracted from reverse the order, so 8 less than a number is \(x - 8\) and never \(8 - x\). When you hit a subtraction phrase, slow down and read it by meaning, asking what is being taken away from what. Remember too that of means multiply, and that is, gives, equals, and results in all become the \(=\) sign that splits a sentence into a full equation.

blocks.x: That dictionary is the whole vocabulary. Every word problem is built from those same few phrases, so nothing genuinely new is coming. What changes is the richness of the situation, how many phrases stack into one story and which quantity the question finally asks for. The next problems run on the same five steps in fuller settings: counts of two different kinds of thing, a choice between two pricing plans, ages a few years apart, and runs of consecutive whole numbers. Same toolkit, bigger rooms.

A puzzle box holds \(30\) tokens, each a square with \(4\) corners or a triangle with \(3\) corners, and all the corners together count \(104\). Let \(s\) be the number of squares, so the triangles are \(30 - s\), the key move being that the second category is the total minus the first. The question asks for triangles. How many triangle tokens are in the box?
hints: You know two totals here, the number of tokens and the number of corners. Pick a letter for one of the counts. Let \(s\) be the number of squares. Since the two kinds add up to \(30\) tokens, how can you write the number of triangles using \(s\)? Then total the corners, \(4\) for each square and \(3\) for each triangle.

The triangles must be \(30 - s\), the total minus the squares. Counting corners gives \(4s + 3(30 - s) = 104\). Distribute and combine like terms to get \(s + 90 = 104\), so \(s = 14\). That is the number of squares, so finish by computing the triangles the question wants, which is \(30 - 14\).
blocks.sol:
Start by naming what you do not yet know. Let \(s\) be the number of square tokens. The box holds \(30\) tokens in all, and every token that is not a square is a triangle, so the number of triangle tokens is \(30 - s\). That is the key move, the second category is the total minus the first.

Now translate the corner count. Each square contributes \(4\) corners and each triangle contributes \(3\) corners, and the grand total is \(104\). That gives the equation $$4s + 3(30 - s) = 104.$$ Distribute the \(3\) to get \(4s + 90 - 3s = 104\). Combine the like terms \(4s\) and \(-3s\) into \(s\), so \(s + 90 = 104\). Subtract \(90\) from both sides to find \(s = 14\), which means there are \(14\) square tokens.

The question did not ask for squares though, it asked for triangles. The triangles are \(30 - s = 30 - 14 = 16\). Read it back against the words to be sure. With \(14\) squares and \(16\) triangles you have \(14 + 16 = 30\) tokens, and the corners come to \(4 \times 14 + 3 \times 16 = 56 + 48 = 104\), exactly as stated. So the box holds \(\boxed{16}\) triangle tokens.

blocks.cap: The shorter piece is \(x\); the longer is \(x+30\). End to end they fill the bar, so \(x+(x+30)=2x+30=150\), giving \(x=60\). The longer piece is \(x+30=90\).

blocks.x: A street magician's riddle gives two descriptions of the same spot. Half her secret number with four shaved off lands exactly where a third of her number with three piled on lands, so \(\tfrac{1}{2}x - 4 = \tfrac{1}{3}x + 3\). Clear the fractions, gather the variable, and solve. What is her secret number?
hints: Let \(x\) stand for the secret number. Now translate each half of the sentence on its own. "Half of my number and shave off four" becomes \(\tfrac{1}{2}x - 4\), and "a third of my number and pile on three" becomes \(\tfrac{1}{3}x + 3\). The phrase "land in exactly the same spot" is the equals sign, so set the two pieces equal.

You have \(\tfrac{1}{2}x - 4 = \tfrac{1}{3}x + 3\), with the variable on both sides and fractions in the way. Clear the fractions by multiplying every single term by \(6\). That turns it into \(3x - 24 = 2x + 18\). Now gather the \(x\) terms on one side and the numbers on the other, and solve.
blocks.sol: Let \(x\) be the magician's secret number. The riddle gives you two descriptions of the same spot, so they must be equal. The first half, "half of my number and shave off four", is \(\tfrac{1}{2}x - 4\). The second half, "a third of my number and pile on three", is \(\tfrac{1}{3}x + 3\). Setting them equal gives the equation \(\tfrac{1}{2}x - 4 = \tfrac{1}{3}x + 3\). The variable sits on both sides and the fractions are awkward, so clear the denominators first by multiplying every term by \(6\). That gives \(3x - 24 = 2x + 18\). Subtract \(2x\) from both sides to get \(x - 24 = 18\), then add \(24\) to both sides to find \(x = 42\). Read it back against the words to be sure. Half of \(42\) is \(21\), and shaving off four leaves \(17\). A third of \(42\) is \(14\), and piling on three gives \(17\). Both land on the same spot, so the secret number is \(\boxed{42}\).

blocks.x: No new tricks, just familiar equations. When a sentence drops part of the number on each side of the word equals, the equation that falls out simply has the variable on both sides, the kind you beat in 6.3, so gather the variable terms and finish. When fractions of the number show up, do not panic: multiply every term by a common multiple to wipe the denominators first, then gather. Translating is just reading carefully enough to write an equation you already know how to solve.

A climbing gym offers two plans. The Steady plan charges a flat \(\$12\) per month plus \(\$0.20\) per session, and the Casual plan charges a flat \(\$4\) per month plus \(\$0.30\) per session. For few sessions Casual is cheaper, for many Steady wins, so somewhere they cost the same. How many sessions in one month make the two plans cost the same amount?
hints: Give the unknown a name. Let \(s\) be the number of sessions in the month. Now write the monthly cost of each plan as an expression in \(s\), and remember that "cost the same" is just an equals sign waiting to happen. To keep everything in whole numbers you can work in cents, so \(\$0.20\) is \(20\) cents and the flat \(\$12\) is \(1200\) cents.

In cents the Steady plan costs \(1200 + 20s\) and the Casual plan costs \(400 + 30s\). Set them equal to get \(1200 + 20s = 400 + 30s\). Get the variable on one side by subtracting \(20s\) from both sides, then subtract \(400\) from both sides, and finish by dividing.
blocks.sol: Start by naming the unknown. Let \(s\) be the number of sessions you book in the month. To stay exact, work in cents, so the flat \(\$12\) is \(1200\) cents and \(\$0.20\) per session is \(20s\) cents. That makes the Steady plan cost \(1200 + 20s\) cents. The Casual plan has a \(\$4\) flat fee, which is \(400\) cents, plus \(\$0.30\) per session, so it costs \(400 + 30s\) cents. The words "cost the same" turn straight into an equals sign, which gives \(1200 + 20s = 400 + 30s\). Subtract \(20s\) from both sides to gather the variable on the right, leaving \(1200 = 400 + 10s\). Subtract \(400\) from both sides to get \(800 = 10s\), and divide both sides by \(10\) to find \(s = 80\). Now read it back against the words. At \(80\) sessions the Steady plan costs \(1200 + 20 \cdot 80 = 2800\) cents and the Casual plan costs \(400 + 30 \cdot 80 = 2800\) cents, and both come out to \(\$28\), so the two plans really do match. The question asked for the number of sessions that make the plans cost the same, which is \(\boxed{80}\).

blocks.cap: The box has \(30\) pieces, so the moment you call the squares \(s\), the triangles have nowhere to hide. They are everything left over, which is \(30 - s\). One letter now describes both groups. Each square brings \(4\) corners and each triangle brings \(3\), so the corners stack up as \(4s\) from the squares and \(3(30 - s)\) from the triangles. Setting that against the \(104\) corners in the box gives \(4s + 3(30 - s) = 104\), a single equation in a single unknown built straight from the picture.

blocks.x: Marisol notices that six years from now her tortoise Pebble will be exactly seven sixths of its current age. Let \(t\) be Pebble's age now, so its future age is \(t + 6\), and that equals \(\tfrac{7}{6}t\). Clear the fraction and solve. How old is Pebble right now, in years?
hints: Pick a letter for the thing you do not know yet, the age right now, and call it \(t\). A future age is just the current age plus however many years pass, so in six years Pebble will be \(t + 6\). The words say that future age equals seven sixths of the age now, so set \(t + 6\) equal to \(\tfrac{7}{6}t\).

Start from \(t + 6 = \tfrac{7}{6}t\). The fraction is annoying, so multiply every single term by \(6\) to clear it, which gives \(6t + 36 = 7t\). Now it is an ordinary equation with the variable on both sides, so subtract \(6t\) from each side and read off \(t\).
blocks.sol: Let \(t\) be Pebble's age right now, in years. That is the number the question is really after. Six years from now Pebble will be six years older, so the future age is \(t + 6\). The words tell us that future age is seven sixths of the current age, which means \(t + 6 = \tfrac{7}{6}t\). To get rid of the fraction, multiply every term by \(6\), giving \(6t + 36 = 7t\). Subtract \(6t\) from both sides and you are left with \(36 = t\), so \(t = 36\). The question asked for Pebble's age now, and that is exactly what \(t\) stands for. Check it against the words. In six years Pebble would be \(36 + 6 = 42\), and seven sixths of \(36\) is \(\tfrac{7}{6}\cdot 36 = 42\) as well, so the future age really does match. Pebble is \(\boxed{36}\) years old now.

blocks.x: About 4,000 years ago Egyptian scribes posed "aha" puzzles: find the heap such that the heap plus a fraction of itself equals a total. All in words, no symbols, yet the logic is exactly this.

Three steel rings have weights that are consecutive even numbers of kilograms and total \(96\) kg. Let the lightest be \(n\), so the three weights are \(n\), \(n + 2\), and \(n + 4\). Set their sum equal to \(96\) and solve for \(n\). The question asks for the heaviest ring, not \(n\). What does the heaviest ring weigh, in kilograms?
hints: Let \(n\) stand for the weight of the lightest ring in kilograms. Because the rings step up by \(2\) each time, the middle ring is \(n + 2\) and the heaviest is \(n + 4\). The three weights add to \(96\), so write that sum as an equation in \(n\).

Your equation is \(n + (n + 2) + (n + 4) = 96\). Combine the like terms on the left to get \(3n + 6 = 96\), then subtract \(6\) and divide by \(3\) to find \(n\). Once you have \(n\), the question wants the heaviest ring, which is \(n + 4\), not \(n\) itself.
blocks.sol: Start by naming the unknown. Let \(n\) be the weight of the lightest ring in kilograms. Since the rings are consecutive even numbers, each one is \(2\) kilograms heavier than the last, so the middle ring weighs \(n + 2\) and the heaviest weighs \(n + 4\). The three weights together read \(96\) kilograms, which turns the words into an equation. $$n + (n + 2) + (n + 4) = 96$$ Combine the like terms on the left. The three copies of \(n\) make \(3n\), and the loose numbers \(2\) and \(4\) make \(6\), so the equation becomes $$3n + 6 = 96.$$ Subtract \(6\) from both sides to get \(3n = 90\), then divide both sides by \(3\) to find \(n = 30\). So the lightest ring is \(30\) kilograms, the middle ring is \(32\), and the heaviest is \(34\). Here is the trap worth naming. The variable \(n\) came out to \(30\), but the question never asked for the lightest ring. It asked for the heaviest, which is \(n + 4 = 34\). Check it against the words. The weights \(30\), \(32\), and \(34\) step up by \(2\) each time and add to \(30 + 32 + 34 = 96\), and \(34\) is indeed the largest of the three. The heaviest ring weighs \(\boxed{34}\) kilograms.

blocks.x: Consecutive integers. When integers run one after another, anchor the first to \(n\) and write the rest as \(n+1\), \(n+2\), and so on, stepping by \(2\) instead for consecutive even or odd integers, so \(n\), \(n+2\), \(n+4\). Add them, set the sum equal to the given total, solve for \(n\), then add the right step to read off the exact member asked for. Always check by reading your answer back into the original sentence, since a misread equation is still satisfied by its own wrong answer.

A baker fills exactly \(40\) boxes, each a small box holding \(6\) cupcakes or a large box holding \(10\), for a total of \(312\) cupcakes. Let \(s\) be the small boxes, so the large boxes are \(40 - s\). Count the cupcakes, solve, then answer for the large boxes. How many large boxes did she fill?
hints: You have two unknowns but they are tied together. Let \(s\) be the number of small boxes. Since every box is small or large and there are \(40\) boxes in total, the number of large boxes must be \(40 - s\). Now count cupcakes, the small boxes contribute \(6s\) and the large boxes contribute \(10\) of them each.

Write the cupcake count as one equation, \(6s + 10(40 - s) = 312\). Distribute and combine like terms to get \(-4s + 400 = 312\). Solve for \(s\), then remember the question asks for large boxes, which is \(40 - s\), not \(s\) itself.
blocks.sol: Let \(s\) be the number of small boxes the baker fills. Every box is either small or large and there are \(40\) boxes altogether, so the two counts add to \(40\) and the number of large boxes is \(40 - s\). That single relationship is what lets one variable carry the whole problem. Now count cupcakes. The small boxes hold \(6\) each, so they account for \(6s\) cupcakes, and the large boxes hold \(10\) each, so they account for \(10(40 - s)\) cupcakes. The total is \(312\), which gives the equation $$6s + 10(40 - s) = 312.$$ Distribute the \(10\) to get \(6s + 400 - 10s = 312\), then combine the like terms \(6s\) and \(-10s\) to get \(-4s + 400 = 312\). Subtract \(400\) from both sides, so \(-4s = -88\), and divide by \(-4\) to find \(s = 22\). So there are \(22\) small boxes. The question did not ask for \(s\), it asked for the large boxes, which is \(40 - s = 40 - 22 = 18\). Check it against the words. With \(22\) small boxes and \(18\) large boxes the total is \(22 + 18 = 40\) boxes, and the cupcakes come to \(6 \cdot 22 + 10 \cdot 18 = 132 + 180 = 312\), both exactly right. The baker filled \(\boxed{18}\) large boxes.

review.x: A magician guessing the number on your card says, four more than triple your number lands exactly on thirty-one. What number is on the card?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number on the card. "Triple your number" is \(3x\), and "four more than" that adds 4, so the left side is \(3x + 4\).
Set \(3x + 4 = 31\). Subtract 4 from both sides, then divide by 3 to find \(x\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number written on the card. Triple the number is \(3x\), and four more than that is \(3x + 4\). The clue says this lands on thirty-one, so the equation is $$3x + 4 = 31.$$ Subtract 4 from both sides to get \(3x = 27\), then divide both sides by 3 to get \(x = 9\). The question asks for the number on the card, which is \(\boxed{9}\). Check against the words. Triple of 9 is 27, and four more than 27 is 31, exactly as the magician said.

review.x: A lighthouse keeper logs ships each night. Wednesday's count was eight less than Tuesday's and came to \(23\), and eight less than Tuesday's number is \(x - 8\). How many ships passed on Tuesday?
hints: Let \(x\) be Tuesday's count. "Eight less than Tuesday's number" means you start at \(x\) and take 8 away, so it is \(x - 8\), not \(8 - x\). The order matters here.
You now have \(x - 8 = 23\). Add 8 to both sides to undo the subtraction and find \(x\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of ships that passed on Tuesday. The phrase "eight less than Tuesday's number" tells you to begin with Tuesday's count and remove 8, which is \(x - 8\). Watch the order, because "less than" flips it. It is \(x - 8\), never \(8 - x\). That Wednesday count equals 23, so the equation is $$x - 8 = 23.$$ Add 8 to both sides to get \(x = 23 + 8\), so \(x = 31\). The question asks for Tuesday's count, and that is exactly the \(x\) we solved for, so the answer is \(\boxed{31}\). Check it against the words. Eight less than 31 is \(31 - 8 = 23\), which matches the Wednesday count, so 31 is correct.

review.x: A museum desk starts the morning with \(50\) maps and \(12\) remain by lunch. The number handed out subtracted from \(50\) leaves \(12\). How many maps were handed out?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number of maps handed out. The maps came off the original fifty, so the fifty comes first and the handed-out amount is subtracted from it. That gives \(50 - x\), and what is left is \(12\). Be careful with the order, it is fifty minus the unknown, not the unknown minus fifty.

You now have \(50 - x = 12\). To get \(x\) by itself, you can add \(x\) to both sides and subtract \(12\) from both sides, which lines up as \(50 - 12 = x\). Finish that subtraction to find the number of maps.
review.sol: Let \(x\) stand for the number of maps handed out during the morning. The phrase that builds the equation is that number subtracted from the fifty, which means the fifty is the starting pile and \(x\) is taken away from it, written as \(50 - x\). What remains is twelve, so the equation is $$50 - x = 12.$$ Solve for \(x\). Add \(x\) to both sides to get \(50 = 12 + x\), then subtract \(12\) from both sides, which gives \(50 - 12 = x\), so \(x = 38\). The question asks how many maps were handed out, and that is exactly the number \(x\), so the answer is \(38\). Check it against the words. Thirty-eight maps subtracted from the original fifty leaves \(50 - 38 = 12\) maps still on the desk, which matches the twelve the guide found. So the number of maps handed out is \(\boxed{38}\).

review.x: Doubling the count of new birdhouses and adding \(7\) gives \(33\). Solve for the count, then find how many would remain if five were taken down. How many birdhouses would be left?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number of birdhouses the crew hung. Doubling it gives \(2x\), and adding seven gives \(2x + 7\). That whole thing equals \(33\), so write \(2x + 7 = 33\).
Solve \(2x + 7 = 33\) to find \(x\). Then reread the question. It does not ask for \(x\) itself, it asks how many would be left if five were taken down, so compute \(x - 5\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of birdhouses the crew hung. Doubling the count gives \(2x\), and adding seven gives \(2x + 7\). The words say this equals thirty-three, so the equation is \(2x + 7 = 33\). Subtract \(7\) from both sides to get \(2x = 26\), then divide by \(2\) to get \(x = 13\). The crew hung \(13\) birdhouses. But the question asks how many would be left if five were taken down, which is \(13 - 5 = 8\). So the answer is \(\boxed{8}\). Check against the words. Double \(13\) to get \(26\), then add \(7\) to get \(33\), which matches "is thirty-three", and \(13 - 5 = 8\) leftover.

review.x: Two bakery trays hold \(45\) croissants together, and the window tray holds \(9\) fewer than the kitchen tray. How many croissants are on the kitchen tray?
hints: Let \(k\) stand for the number of croissants on the kitchen tray. The window tray holds 9 fewer, so write that as \(k - 9\).
The two trays together hold 45, so \(k + (k - 9) = 45\). Combine the like terms and solve for \(k\).
review.sol: Let \(k\) be the number of croissants on the kitchen tray. The window tray holds 9 fewer than the kitchen tray, so it holds \(k - 9\). The two trays together hold 45, which gives the equation $$k + (k - 9) = 45.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(2k - 9 = 45\). Add 9 to both sides to get \(2k = 54\), then divide by 2 to get \(k = 27\). The question asks for the kitchen tray, which is exactly the variable, so the answer is \(\boxed{27}\). Check it against the words. The kitchen tray has 27, the window tray has \(27 - 9 = 18\), and together they hold \(27 + 18 = 45\), which matches.

review.x: A llama drinks four times as much water as a goat, and together they drink \(40\) liters. How many liters did the llama drink?
hints: Let \(g\) stand for the liters the goat drank. The llama drank four times as much, so the llama is \(4g\). Write an equation that says the two amounts add up to \(40\).
Your equation is \(g + 4g = 40\). Combine the like terms into \(5g\), solve for \(g\), and then remember the question asks about the llama, not the goat.
review.sol: Let \(g\) be the number of liters the goat drank. The llama drank four times as much, so the llama drank \(4g\). Together they drank \(40\) liters, which gives the equation $$g + 4g = 40.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(5g = 40\), then divide both sides by \(5\) to find \(g = 8\). That is the goat's amount, but the question asks how much the llama drank, so report \(4g = 4 \times 8 = 32\) liters. Check it against the words. The goat drank \(8\) liters and the llama drank \(32\) liters, the llama's \(32\) is four times the goat's \(8\), and \(8 + 32 = 40\), which matches the total. The llama drank \(\boxed{32}\) liters.

review.x: A robotics club builds \(26\) machines, each a roller with \(4\) wheels or a hopper with \(2\) wheels, totaling \(84\) wheels. How many hoppers did they build?
hints: Let \(c\) be the number of rollers. Then the number of hoppers is whatever is left out of 26, which is \(26 - c\). Rollers carry 4 wheels each and hoppers carry 2 wheels each.

Write the total wheels as \(4c + 2(26 - c) = 84\). Expand the left side and combine like terms, then solve for \(c\). Once you know \(c\), the question asks for hoppers, so report \(26 - c\), not \(c\) itself.
review.sol: Let \(c\) be the number of rollers. Since every machine is a roller or a hopper and there are 26 in all, the number of hoppers is \(26 - c\). Each roller has 4 wheels and each hopper has 2 wheels, so the wheel count is \(4c + 2(26 - c) = 84\). Expand the left side to get \(4c + 52 - 2c = 84\), which simplifies to \(2c + 52 = 84\). Subtract 52 from both sides to get \(2c = 32\), so \(c = 16\). That is the number of rollers, but the question asks for hoppers, and the hoppers are \(26 - c = 26 - 16 = 10\). So the club built \(\boxed{10}\) hoppers. Check it against the words. With 16 rollers and 10 hoppers that is \(16 + 10 = 26\) machines, and the wheels come to \(4 \times 16 + 2 \times 10 = 64 + 20 = 84\), which matches both counts.

review.x: A satellite finds that a quarter of the ice crystal count plus \(5\) equals a sixth of the count plus \(9\). How many ice crystals are in the cloud?
hints: Let \(x\) be the number of ice crystals. A quarter of the count and add five is \(\frac{1}{4}x + 5\), and a sixth of the count and add nine is \(\frac{1}{6}x + 9\). Set them equal.
To get rid of the fractions, multiply every single term by \(12\), the smallest number both \(4\) and \(6\) divide into. That turns \(\frac{1}{4}x\) into \(3x\) and \(\frac{1}{6}x\) into \(2x\).
review.sol: Let \(x\) be the number of ice crystals in the cloud. A quarter of the count increased by five is \(\frac{1}{4}x + 5\), and a sixth of the count increased by nine is \(\frac{1}{6}x + 9\). The words say these are equal, so \(\frac{1}{4}x + 5 = \frac{1}{6}x + 9\). Multiply every term by \(12\) to clear the fractions, which gives \(3x + 60 = 2x + 108\). Subtract \(2x\) from both sides to get \(x + 60 = 108\), then subtract \(60\) to get \(x = 48\). The question asks for the number of ice crystals, so the answer is \(\boxed{48}\). Check against the words. A quarter of \(48\) is \(12\), and \(12 + 5 = 17\). A sixth of \(48\) is \(8\), and \(8 + 9 = 17\). Both sides give \(17\), so it fits.

review.x: A club buys \(12\) glow items, each a stick at \(\$3\) or a lantern at \(\$5\), spending \(\$44\) in all. How many glow lanterns did they buy?
hints: Let \(p\) be the number of glow sticks. Since there are \(12\) items in all, the number of lanterns has to be \(12 - p\). Each glow stick costs \(\$3\) and each lantern costs \(\$5\), and the whole total is \(\$44\), so write \(3p + 5(12 - p) = 44\).
Distribute the \(5\) to get \(3p + 60 - 5p = 44\), which simplifies to \(-2p + 60 = 44\). Solve that for \(p\), then remember the question asks for lanterns, which is \(12 - p\), not \(p\) itself.
review.sol: Let \(p\) be the number of glow sticks the club buys. There are \(12\) items in all, so the number of lanterns is \(12 - p\). Each glow stick costs \(\$3\) and each lantern costs \(\$5\), and the total spent is \(\$44\), which gives the equation $$3p + 5(12 - p) = 44.$$ Distribute the \(5\) across the group, \(5(12 - p) = 60 - 5p\), so the equation becomes \(3p + 60 - 5p = 44\). Combine the \(p\) terms, \(3p - 5p = -2p\), leaving \(-2p + 60 = 44\). Subtract \(60\) from both sides to get \(-2p = -16\), then divide both sides by \(-2\) to find \(p = 8\). So the club bought \(8\) glow sticks. The question asks for lanterns, not glow sticks, and the number of lanterns is \(12 - p = 12 - 8 = 4\). Check it against the words. With \(8\) glow sticks and \(4\) lanterns the count is \(8 + 4 = 12\) items, and the cost is \(3 \cdot 8 + 5 \cdot 4 = 24 + 20 = 44\) dollars, both of which match. So the club bought \(\boxed{4}\) glow lanterns.

review.x: Four years ago a boat was two thirds of its current age, so with \(t\) its age now, \(t - 4 = \tfrac{2}{3}t\). How old is the boat now, in years?
hints: The equation \(t - 4 = \tfrac{2}{3}t\) has a fraction in it. Multiplying every single term on both sides by \(3\) will clear that fraction and leave you whole numbers to work with.
review.sol: Let \(t\) be the boat's age in years today. Four years ago its age was \(t - 4\), and the plaque says that equals two thirds of its current age, which gives \(t - 4 = \tfrac{2}{3}t\). Clear the fraction by multiplying every term by \(3\), so \(3t - 12 = 2t\). Subtract \(2t\) from both sides to get \(t - 12 = 0\), and then \(t = 12\). The question asks how old the boat is now, and the variable \(t\) is exactly that age, so the answer is \(\boxed{12}\) years. Check it against the words. Four years ago the boat was \(12 - 4 = 8\) years old, and two thirds of its current age is \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times 12 = 8\). Those match, so \(12\) is right.

review.x: Priya and Dev's ages add to \(44\) years, and Priya is exactly three times as old as Dev. How old is Priya, in years?
hints: Let \(y\) stand for Dev's age, the younger one. Then Priya's age is \(3y\), since she is three times as old. Their ages add to \(44\).
Write \(y + 3y = 44\) and combine the like terms to get \(4y = 44\). Solve for \(y\), then remember the question asks for Priya, not Dev.
review.sol: Let \(y\) be Dev's age, the younger cousin. Priya is three times as old, so her age is \(3y\). Their ages add to \(44\), which gives the equation $$y + 3y = 44.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(4y = 44\), and divide both sides by \(4\) to find \(y = 11\). So Dev is \(11\). The question asks for Priya, whose age is \(3y = 3 \cdot 11 = 33\). Check it against the words. The ages add to \(11 + 33 = 44\), and \(33\) is three times \(11\), so both conditions hold. Priya is \(\boxed{33}\) years old.

review.x: Three hallway panels have consecutive integer widths in tiles that sum to \(72\). How many tiles wide is the widest panel?
hints: The three panel widths are consecutive integers. If you call the narrowest one \(n\), the next is \(n+1\) and the widest is \(n+2\).

Add the three widths and set the total equal to 72. That gives \(n + (n+1) + (n+2) = 72\), which simplifies to \(3n + 3 = 72\). Solve for \(n\) first, then remember the question asks for the widest panel, not the narrowest.
review.sol: Let \(n\) be the width of the narrowest panel in tiles. Each panel is one tile wider than the one before it, so the three widths are \(n\), \(n+1\), and \(n+2\). Together they span 72 tiles, so \(n + (n+1) + (n+2) = 72\). Combine the like terms to get \(3n + 3 = 72\). Subtract 3 from both sides for \(3n = 69\), then divide by 3 to get \(n = 23\). The narrowest panel is 23 tiles, the middle one is 24 tiles, and the widest is \(n + 2 = 25\) tiles. The question asks for the widest panel, so the answer is \(\boxed{25}\). Check it against the words. The three widths 23, 24, and 25 each grow by one tile, and \(23 + 24 + 25 = 72\), which matches the total span.

review.x: Two padlock codes are consecutive odd numbers that sum to \(56\). What is the larger of the two codes?
hints: Consecutive odd numbers always sit two apart, so if the smaller one is \(n\), the next odd one is \(n + 2\).
Add the two codes and set the sum equal to \(56\), giving \(n + (n + 2) = 56\). Solve for \(n\), then remember the question asks for the larger code, which is \(n + 2\).
review.sol: Let \(n\) be the smaller code. Because the two codes are consecutive odd numbers, they are two apart, so the larger code is \(n + 2\). The two codes add to \(56\), which gives the equation \(n + (n + 2) = 56\). Combining like terms, \(2n + 2 = 56\). Subtract \(2\) from both sides to get \(2n = 54\), then divide by \(2\) to find \(n = 27\). The smaller code is \(27\) and the larger code is \(n + 2 = 29\). The question asks for the larger code, so the answer is \(\boxed{29}\). Check against the words. The codes \(27\) and \(29\) are both odd, they are consecutive odd numbers since they differ by \(2\), and \(27 + 29 = 56\), which matches the total.

review.x: Garage A charges \(\$5\) plus \(\$2\) per hour, and Garage B charges \(\$11\) plus \(\$1\) per hour. At how many hours of parking do the two garages cost the same?
hints: Let \(h\) be the number of hours. Garage A costs \(5 + 2h\) dollars and Garage B costs \(11 + h\) dollars. The two charge the same when those expressions are equal.
Set \(5 + 2h = 11 + h\). Subtract \(h\) from both sides to gather the variable, then subtract \(5\) to get \(h\) by itself.
review.sol: Let \(h\) be the number of hours parked. Garage A costs \(5 + 2h\) dollars and Garage B costs \(11 + h\) dollars. The two garages charge the same amount when those two costs are equal, so \(5 + 2h = 11 + h\). Subtract \(h\) from both sides to get \(5 + h = 11\), then subtract \(5\) from both sides to get \(h = 6\). The question asks at how many hours the costs match, which is exactly \(h\), so the answer is \(\boxed{6}\) hours. Check it against the words. At \(6\) hours Garage A costs \(5 + 2\cdot 6 = \$17\) and Garage B costs \(11 + 6 = \$17\), and the two amounts agree.

blocks.x: Every equation so far ended on one exact value, the single number that makes both sides match. Real life is rarely that tidy. A recipe wants the oven at least \(350\) degrees, so \(351\) and \(400\) work too, and a bridge bars trucks above a posted height, so countless heights pass. Each answer is a whole stretch of the number line, called an inequality. You already own the tools: the same balance moves from 6.1 through 6.4, with exactly one new twist waiting near the end.

A solo ride only lets you board if your ticket count is strictly more than \(5\), written \(t > 5\). The word strictly matters, because holding exactly \(5\) is not enough, you have to clear the line, not land on it. Five friends hold \(3\), \(5\), \(7\), \(12\), and \(5.5\) tickets. How many of those counts satisfy \(t > 5\)? Type the single number.
hints: The rule is \(t > 5\). Go through the friends one at a time and ask whether each ticket count is bigger than \(5\). Watch the friend holding exactly \(5\), since \(> 5\) does not include \(5\) itself.
Mark each value yes or no for \(t > 5\). \(3\) is no, \(5\) is no because it only ties the line, \(7\) is yes, \(12\) is yes, and \(5.5\) is yes. Count the yeses.
blocks.sol: The rule is already solved for \(t\), so there is no rearranging to do and no sign to flip. The condition is simply \(t > 5\), which in words means the ticket count must be larger than \(5\), and the strict symbol \(>\) means \(5\) itself is left out. Now test each friend against \(t > 5\). For \(t = 3\), is \(3 > 5\)? No. For \(t = 5\), is \(5 > 5\)? No, because \(5\) is equal to \(5\), not greater, and strictly more means you cannot sit on the boundary. For \(t = 7\), is \(7 > 5\)? Yes. For \(t = 12\), is \(12 > 5\)? Yes. For \(t = 5.5\), is \(5.5 > 5\)? Yes, since half a ticket past \(5\) still clears the line. The solution range is every value above \(5\), written \(t > 5\), and the friend at exactly \(5\) falls just outside it. Counting the yeses gives \(7\), \(12\), and \(5.5\), which is three friends. So the number allowed to ride alone is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: Inequality basics. An equals sign swears the two sides are the same number, pinning you to one answer, but an inequality only swears one side outranks the other, so a whole band of numbers can make it true at once. Keep four symbols straight: \(>\) and \(<\) are strict, when the sides may never tie, while \(\ge\) and \(\le\) let the boundary itself join. Because a range works instead of one number, the picture is a ray sliding off in one direction, not a single dot.

blocks.cap: A hollow circle and a filled circle say two different things at the endpoint. The top line graphs \(x > 3\), so the circle at \(3\) is hollow because the sign is strict and \(3\) itself is left out, while the ray sweeps right over every value above \(3\) at once. The bottom line graphs \(x \le 7\), so the circle at \(7\) is filled because the sign allows equality and \(7\) is kept in, while the ray sweeps left through \(0\) and on over every value below \(7\). Same idea both times, a hollow ring drops the boundary and a filled dot holds onto it, and the arrow shades in the whole direction in one stroke.

blocks.x: Reading the graph. The little circle at the end is a gate. A hollow open circle means that exact number is locked out, the case for strict \(>\) and \(<\); you can creep as close as you like but never land on it. A filled closed circle swings the gate open and lets the endpoint in, since the or-equal part of \(\ge\) and \(\le\) says equality counts. The arrow finishes the story, carrying every value that works off in its direction.

A delivery cart already holds \(8\) crates, and the running total must stay under \(20\), so with \(x\) more crates the rule is \(x + 8 < 20\). Solve it the same way as \(x + 8 = 20\), by subtracting \(8\) from both sides, with no sign flip for subtraction. The boundary is strict, so a value sitting exactly on it does not count. What is the largest whole number \(x\) can be?
hints: Make the first move you would make for an equation. Subtract \(8\) from both sides to get \(x\) alone. Adding or subtracting the same amount never flips an inequality, so the \(<\) sign stays pointing the same way. Then notice the bound is strict, which matters for a whole number answer.

Subtracting \(8\) from both sides gives \(x < 12\). Since \(x\) must be strictly less than \(12\), the value \(12\) itself is out. The largest whole number that still fits is the integer just below \(12\).
blocks.sol: Start with the rule from the problem, \(x + 8 < 20\). To get \(x\) by itself, subtract \(8\) from both sides, exactly the move you would make for the equation \(x + 8 = 20\). Subtracting the same amount from both sides shifts the values but never reverses the inequality, so there is no sign flip here and the \(<\) keeps pointing the same way. That leaves \(x < 20 - 8\), which is \(x < 12\). In words, the solution is every value strictly less than \(12\), written \(x < 12\). The bound is strict, so \(12\) is not allowed, you would need fewer than \(12\) more crates. The largest whole number strictly below \(12\) is \(11\). Check it. Loading \(11\) more crates gives \(11 + 8 = 19\), and \(19 < 20\) is true, while \(12\) more would give \(12 + 8 = 20\), and \(20 < 20\) is false, so \(11\) really is the top. The most crates you may stack on is \(\boxed{11}\).

blocks.x: A diver's gauge reads negatively below the surface. Her reading is \(x\), she rises \(5\) meters to \(x + 5\), and the safety check needs the new reading at least \(2\), so \(x + 5 \ge 2\). Subtract \(5\) from both sides; subtracting never flips the sign. Because the sign is \(\ge\), the boundary itself is allowed. What is the smallest value \(x\) can be?
hints: Treat the inequality just like an equation for the first move. To get \(x\) by itself, undo the \(+5\) by subtracting 5 from both sides. You only flip the inequality sign when you multiply or divide both sides by a negative, and subtracting does not do that, so the \(\ge\) stays pointing the same way.

Subtract 5 from both sides of \(x + 5 \ge 2\) and you get \(x \ge -3\). The sign is \(\ge\), which means \(-3\) itself is allowed, so the gate is closed. The smallest reading that still works is the boundary number \(-3\).
blocks.sol: Start with the safety condition \(x + 5 \ge 2\). You solve it exactly the way you solved equations all chapter, by undoing operations one at a time. Undo the \(+5\) by subtracting 5 from both sides, which gives \(x \ge -3\). No sign flip happens here, because the sign only reverses when you multiply or divide both sides by a negative, and subtracting a number never does that, so the \(\ge\) keeps pointing the same way. In words, her starting reading can be \(-3\) or anything greater. In symbols that is \(x \ge -3\). Because the sign is \(\ge\) and not \(>\), the boundary \(-3\) is itself allowed, so the gate is closed and \(-3\) is a value she is permitted to start from. The smallest value \(x\) can be is therefore the boundary number, \(-3\). $$\boxed{-3}$$

blocks.x: Four identical sandbags sit on a shelf rated for at most \(26\) kilograms total, so with one bag weighing \(x\), the rule is \(4x \le 26\). Solve it like an equation, keeping the inequality sign: divide both sides by the positive number \(4\), which does not flip the sign. The word most means the boundary counts. What is the largest value \(x\) can be? Give a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The bags share the load, so undo the \(4\) by dividing both sides by \(4\). You are dividing by a positive number here, so watch what happens to the inequality sign. When you divide by a positive, the sign keeps pointing the same way, it does not flip.

Divide both sides of \(4x \le 26\) by \(4\). The sign stays as \(\le\), giving \(x \le \tfrac{26}{4}\). Simplify \(\tfrac{26}{4}\) to lowest terms, and since the bound is "at most" the boundary itself is allowed, so the largest value is that boundary.
blocks.sol: Start from the setup \(4x \le 26\). The four bags together cannot top \(26\) kilograms, so their combined weight \(4x\) is at most \(26\). To isolate \(x\), divide both sides by \(4\). Because \(4\) is a positive number, the inequality keeps pointing the same direction, so there is no sign flip here. $$\frac{4x}{4} \le \frac{26}{4}$$ That leaves \(x \le \tfrac{26}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{26}{4}\) reduces to \(\tfrac{13}{2}\). So the solution range is every weight up to and including \(\tfrac{13}{2}\), in words "x is at most thirteen halves" and in symbols \(x \le \tfrac{13}{2}\). The word "most" means the boundary counts, the gate is closed, so \(\tfrac{13}{2}\) itself is allowed. The largest value \(x\) can be is therefore the boundary, \(\boxed{13/2}\) kilograms.

blocks.x: The same solving moves carry over. Solving an inequality is the same game as solving an equation, just with a \(>\) or \(<\) where the \(=\) used to be. Tidy each side, undo a plus with a minus, undo a times with a divide, and the bigger side stays the bigger side, because adding or scaling both sides by the same positive amount moves them together. Every move from 6.2 and 6.3 carries straight over. There is exactly one move not tried yet, scaling by a negative, waiting in the next problem.
The symbols \(<\) and \(>\) first appeared in Thomas Harriot's 1631 book. The wide end faces the larger value. The rounded cousins \(\le\) and \(\ge\) were popularized by Pierre Bouguer in the 1700s.

Here is a statement nobody would argue with: \(3 < 8\). Now multiply both sides by \(-1\), so the \(3\) becomes \(-3\) and the \(8\) becomes \(-8\). On a number line, the one farther left is the smaller, so \(-8\) is now the smaller value. Of the two results \(-3\) and \(-8\), which one is the larger? Type that value.
hints: Put \(-3\) and \(-8\) on a number line and remember the rule from earlier chapters. Farther to the left means smaller, farther to the right means larger. Watch what happened to the order. The statement started as \(3 < 8\), with the left number smaller, but after the move the smaller one is no longer on the left.
Mark both numbers. \(-8\) is eight steps left of zero, and \(-3\) is only three steps left of zero, so \(-3\) sits to the RIGHT of \(-8\). The number on the right is the larger one. That is \(-3\).
blocks.sol: Start with a true statement, \(3 < 8\). Multiply both sides by \(-1\). The left side becomes \(-3\) and the right side becomes \(-8\). Here is the surprise. If you just copied the old direction you would write \(-3 < -8\), but that is false, because \(-8\) is farther left on the number line and is the smaller number. To keep the statement true the inequality sign has to flip. This is the sign flip. Multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative reverses the direction of the inequality, so the true statement is \(-3 > -8\). Reflecting both numbers across zero turned the order around. In words, after the flip \(-3\) is now the larger value and \(-8\) is the smaller one, and the relationship is \(-3 > -8\). The question asks for the larger of the two results, which is \(\boxed{-3}\).

blocks.cap: Start with something plainly true, \(3 < 8\). Multiplying both sides by \(-1\) sends every number to its opposite, which on the line is a reflection straight through zero. The point at \(3\) lands on \(-3\) and the point at \(8\) lands on \(-8\). But a reflection turns left into right, so \(8\), once the rightmost and largest, becomes \(-8\), now the leftmost and smallest. To keep the statement true the relationship has to read \(-3 > -8\). That single forced swap, from \(<\) to \(>\), is the whole reason the inequality sign flips whenever you multiply or divide both sides by a negative.

blocks.x: The one new rule. The moment you multiply or divide both sides by a negative number, you must flip the inequality, so \(<\) becomes \(>\), \(\le\) becomes \(\ge\), and the same the other way, because crossing to the negative side reverses which value is bigger. Adding, subtracting, and scaling by a positive never flip anything, so those moves are always safe. The whole skill comes down to one question at the very end: did I just divide by a negative?

A freezer alarm trips whenever \(-5x\) climbs above \(17\), so the danger zone is \(-5x > 17\). To free \(x\), divide both sides by \(-5\), and dividing by a negative flips the \(>\) to \(<\). What is the largest integer \(x\) can be?
hints: Get \(x\) alone by dividing both sides by \(-5\). The moment you divide by a negative number, something has to happen to the inequality sign. Decide what it becomes before you read off the answer.

Dividing by \(-5\) flips \(>\) into \(<\), so \(x < -\frac{17}{5}\), which is \(x < -3.4\). The gate at \(-3.4\) is open, so \(-3.4\) itself is not allowed. Now name the largest integer that sits strictly to the left of \(-3.4\) on the number line.
blocks.sol: Start with \(-5x > 17\). To free \(x\), divide both sides by \(-5\). Here is the one twist that makes inequalities different from equations. Dividing both sides by a negative number reverses the inequality, so the \(>\) flips to \(<\). $$x < \frac{17}{-5} = -\frac{17}{5} = -3.4$$ In words, \(x\) must be smaller than negative three and four tenths. In symbols that is \(x < -\frac{17}{5}\). The bound is strict, so the gate at \(-\frac{17}{5}\) is open and \(-3.4\) itself is shut out. The question asks for the largest integer that fits. Walking left from \(-3.4\) on the number line, the first integer you reach is \(-4\), since \(-3\) sits to the right of \(-3.4\) and is too big. A quick check, \(-5 \cdot (-4) = 20\), and \(20 > 17\) is true, while \(-5 \cdot (-3) = 15\), and \(15 > 17\) is false. The largest integer \(x\) can be is \(\boxed{-4}\).

blocks.x: This one pulls everything together: the variable appears on both sides and there is a group to clear. Solve \(3(2 - x) > 2x + 1\) by distributing, then gathering the variable, and stay alert because one step divides by a negative and forces the sign to flip. Once you have the range, what is the largest integer \(x\) can be?
hints: Start exactly the way you would for an equation. Distribute the \(3\) across the parentheses to get \(6 - 3x\) on the left, then move the variable terms to one side and the numbers to the other. Keep one eye out for a moment where you divide by a negative, since that is the only thing here that behaves differently from an equation.

After distributing you have \(6 - 3x > 2x + 1\). Subtract \(2x\) from both sides to reach \(6 - 5x > 1\), then subtract \(6\) to get \(-5x > -5\). Now divide both sides by \(-5\), and because that divisor is negative, FLIP the sign. That gives \(x < 1\). The bound is strict, so \(1\) itself is not allowed. Name the largest whole number that still sits below \(1\).
blocks.sol: Treat this like any both-sides equation right up until the very last step. First distribute the \(3\) across the parentheses. $$3(2 - x) > 2x + 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 6 - 3x > 2x + 1$$ Now gather the variable terms. Subtract \(2x\) from both sides. $$6 - 5x > 1$$ Subtract \(6\) from both sides to isolate the variable term. $$-5x > -5$$ Here is the one move that makes inequalities different. To get \(x\) by itself you divide both sides by \(-5\), and dividing by a negative number FLIPS the inequality sign. The \(>\) becomes \(<\). $$x < 1$$ In words, every number strictly less than \(1\) makes the original statement true, and \(1\) itself does not, because the bound is strict. In symbols the solution is \(x < 1\). The question asks for the largest integer \(x\) can be. Since \(1\) is shut out by the strict sign, the largest integer that still fits is the whole number just below it, which is \(0\). A quick check confirms it. Putting \(x = 0\) into the original gives \(3(2) = 6\) on the left and \(1\) on the right, and \(6 > 1\) is true, while \(x = 1\) gives \(3\) on the left and \(3\) on the right, and \(3 > 3\) is false. The largest integer is \(\boxed{0}\).

blocks.x: The trickiest one, folding everything together. Solve \(5 - 2x - 8 > 4 - x\). The left side has two number pieces sitting apart, so tidy it first, then gather the variables. One move near the end forces the sign to turn around. Once you have the range, what is the largest integer \(x\) can be?
hints: Start by combining the two number terms on the left, since \(5 - 8\) is a single value. That turns the left side into \(-3 - 2x\). Then gather the variables on one side the same way you did when solving equations. Keep an eye out for the step that might flip the inequality.

After tidying you have \(-3 - 2x > 4 - x\). Add \(x\) to both sides to get \(-3 - x > 4\), then add \(3\) to both sides to get \(-x > 7\). Now multiply both sides by \(-1\), and because the multiplier is negative you must reverse the sign, giving \(x < -7\). The bound is strict, so \(-7\) itself is not allowed. The largest integer that fits is the whole number just below \(-7\).
blocks.sol: Begin by tidying the left side. The numbers \(5\) and \(-8\) combine, and \(5 - 8 = -3\), so the inequality becomes \(-3 - 2x > 4 - x\). Now gather the variables. Add \(x\) to both sides, and \(-2x + x = -x\), which gives \(-3 - x > 4\). Add \(3\) to both sides to clear the number on the left, leaving \(-x > 7\). The last move is to turn \(-x\) into \(x\) by multiplying both sides by \(-1\). Multiplying by a negative reverses the inequality, so the sign flips and the result is \(x < -7\). In words, \(x\) is any number strictly less than negative seven, written \(x < -7\). The gate at \(-7\) is open, so \(-7\) does not count, and the largest integer below it is \(-8\). A quick check confirms it, since putting \(x = -8\) into the original gives \(13 > 12\), which is true, while \(x = -7\) gives \(11 > 11\), which is false. So the largest integer \(x\) can be is \(\boxed{-8}\).
